question: What is the UPES Policy & Governance Manual?
answer: It's the official document containing all University-level academic policies. Think of it as your complete academic rulebook that covers everything from grading to graduation. What it does: Ensures consistency across all Schools Clearly separates policies from operational procedures Serves as the single source of truth for academic decisions Aligns with UGC regulations and NEP 2020

question: Who does this manual apply to?
answer: ALL students enrolled in: Undergraduate programmes (3-year, 4-year, 5-year) Postgraduate programmes All Schools and Departments at UPES

question: What is the Policy Hierarchy?
answer: Documents at UPES are ranked in order of authority: Rank Document Type What It Is Authority Level 1 University Policies Official rules approved by Academic Council HIGHEST - Overrides everything else 2 SOPs (Standard Operating Procedures) Step-by-step implementation guides Must align with policies 3 Guidelines/Frameworks Advisory documents Interpretative only 4 Forms/Templates/Checklists Transaction tools No rule-making power 5 Student FAQs Simplified explanations For understanding only Key Rule: If there's any conflict between these levels, University Policies always win. Example: If an SOP s

question: What is "Progression with Accountability"?
answer: You can move forward semester-wise , but you must address academic deficiencies (backlogs) within defined timelines in case backlogs are equal to or more than 50% in an academic year. What this means: You don't have to clear everything to progress But you can't ignore backlogs forever You must use approved recovery pathways

question: What is the "Recovery-First Philosophy"?
answer: UPES prioritizes helping you clear failed courses over taking severe actions. In practice: Multiple recovery options available (Supplementary, Summer, DR, Regular Repeat) Clearing backlogs is more important than taking new courses Academic support comes before academic penalties However: Recovery doesn't mean "easy." Recovery pathways are actually MORE rigorous than regular courses.

question: What does "Academic Rigour" mean?
answer: Flexibility in progression doesn't mean lower standards. Key points: Learning outcomes remain non-negotiable Degree quality is protected Recovery options maintain academic standards You still need to demonstrate competency

question: What is "Student Choice with Responsibility"?
answer: You have choices in your academic path, but you're responsible for the consequences. Examples: You can choose Summer Semester OR Directed Reading—but you must complete it You can change your Major—but you accept potential credit loss 6 You can take a Break of Study—but it counts toward maximum duration

question: What is "Transparency & Predictability"?
answer: All rules are clearly stated, and they apply to everyone equally. Guarantees: No secret rules No arbitrary decisions Same rules for everyone in the same situation Clear consequences announced in advance

question: What is "Completion Within Prescribed Duration"?
answer: All progression and recovery must happen within UGC's maximum time limit. The limit: N+2+1. 1.3. MAXIMUM PROGRAMME DURATION (N+1+2)

question: What is N+2+1?
answer: The maximum time allowed to complete your degree: N = Normal programme duration +2 = Two additional academic years allowed +1 = One more year with Vice Chancellor's special approval Programme-wise breakdown: Programme Type Normal (N) Maximum (N+2) Extended Max (N+2+1) 3-year UG 3 years 5 years 6 years 4-year UG / Honours 4 years 6 years 7 years 5-year Integrated 5 years 7 years 8 years 1-year PG 1 year 3 years 4 years 2-year PG 2 years 4 years 5 years 7

question: What counts toward this duration?
answer: EVERYTHING counts: Regular semesters Summer semesters Break of Study periods Academic Year Back Time spent before changing major or specialisation Nothing is "free time."

question: What happens if I exceed N+2+1?
answer: You cannot continue the programme. Your options: Exit with whatever credits you've earned (Certificate/Diploma under NEP 2020) Discontinue without a degree This is a hard limit. Plan accordingly.

question: Does Break of Study count toward N+2+1?
answer: YES. Every semester of break counts toward your maximum duration. Example: 4-year BTech student takes 1 year break: Normal: 4 years Break: 1 year used Remaining time: 6-1 = 5 years maximum 1.4. CREDIT REQUIREMENTS

question: What is a "credit"?
answer: A unit that measures academic workload. Generally: 1 credit = 1 hour of lecture per week for the semester 1 credit = 2 hours of lab/practical per week 8

question: How many credits must I earn per year?
answer: Minimum 40 credits per academic year (on an average) for all programmes. Why this matters: Ensures you're on track for timely completion Falling below 40 credits may need a student to complete backlogs through academic recovery pathways

question: Can a programme require MORE than the minimum credits?
answer: Yes, but with conditions: Must be academically justified Requires Board of Studies approval Requires Academic Council approval Must be documented Example: An engineering programme might require 167 credits instead of 160, if justified.

question: What is the maximum credits I can take in one semester?
answer: 27 credits is the hard limit for regular semesters. Additional rules: Summer Semester credits don't count toward this 27-credit limit Credit load beyond 27 credits is permissible for certain programmes governed by PCI/BCI with Academic Council approval. 9

question: What is a typical semester load?
answer: Most students take 20-24 credits per semester to spread the workload reasonably. Planning tip: 40 credits/year ÷ 2 semesters = 20 credits/semester minimum Most programmes recommend 22-24 credits/semester 1.5. WHERE TO GET HELP?

question: Where can I find these policies?
answer: Official sources: UPES Website People who can help: Your Programme Leader/ Cluster Head Dean's office Write us at: upespolicies@upes.ac.in

question: What if I don't understand a policy?
answer: Follow this: Step Who to Contact When 1 Program Leader/ Cluster Head First point of contact for clarification 2 Academic Mentor If you need interpretation or advice 3 APO Office/ COE Office For official clarification or written confirmation 4 Dean's Office For complex situations or appeals

question: What if a policy doesn't cover my situation?
answer: Raise it with your Cluster Head They may escalate to the Dean Dean may exercise discretion or refer to competent authority University may create an exception (rare, documented) Important: Absence of a rule doesn't mean automatic approval. 10 SECTION 2: ACADEMIC PROGRESSIN FRAMEWORK (AG-01) 11 2.1. UNDERSTANDING ACADEMIC PROGRESSION

question: What is "academic progression"?
answer: Your movement through the programme, measured by: Course-level completion (passing individual courses) Semester-wise progression (moving from Sem 1 → Sem 2 → Sem 3, etc.) Credit accumulation (building up toward the 120/160/200 total) Programme completion (earning your degree)

question: What is "Continuous Academic Progression"?
answer: UPES follows a system where you normally progress semester-wise even with backlogs. Key features: Backlogs don't automatically stop progression You can move to Semester 2 even with failed courses from Semester 1 BUT you must clear backlogs through Academic Recovery Pathways Subject to attendance and prerequisite requirements Example: Semester 1: You fail 2 out of 8 courses You still move to Semester 2 You take Semester 2 courses + work on clearing those 2 backlogs

question: Do I need to pass everything to progress to the next semester?
answer: No, not everything. But there are limits: You CAN progress with some failed courses (backlogs) But you need to be mindful: You fail prerequisite courses it will blocks dependent courses You fail 50% or more credits in an academic year it triggers Academic Year Back 12

question: What are "Academic Recovery Pathways" (ARP)?
answer: Official mechanisms to clear failed courses: Pathway What It Is When to Use Supplementary Exam Re-take End-Term only After failing with ≥75% attendance Summer Semester Intensive term in summer After debarment OR failed Supplementary Directed Reading (DR) Faculty-supervised self-study Limited availability Regular Repeat Full course repeat with next batch One of recovery pathways as well as mandatory when other options are exhausted Course Substitution Replace elective with another Electives only Academic Year Back Structured recovery year Mandatory if fail ≥50% annual credits

question: What happens if I don't clear backlogs?
answer: Consequences escalate: Short term: Can't register for courses needing those as prerequisites Medium term: May trigger Academic Year Back if backlogs are for more than 50% credits Long term: Cannot graduate until ALL required credits are cleared, chances to breach maximum duration limit, must clear everything within N+2+1 or exit without degree 2.2 GRADING SYSTEM

question: What is the minimum grade to pass a course?
answer: Undergraduate: Grade C (35% minimum marks) Postgraduate: Grade C (40% minimum marks) Critical: Getting below C means F (Fail).

question: What is SGPA?
answer: Semester Grade Point Average - Your performance in one semester. Formula: SGPA = Σ(Grade Point × Credits) ÷ Total Credits in semester Example Calculation: Course Grade Grade Point Credits Points × Credits Math A+ 9 4 36 Physics A 8 4 32 Programming B+ 7 4 28 English O 10 3 30 Lab B 6 2 12 Total 17 138 SGPA = 138 ÷ 17 = 8.12

question: What is CGPA?
answer: Cumulative Grade Point Average - Your overall performance across all semesters. Formula: CGPA = Σ(SGPA × Semester Credits) ÷ Total Credits earned Example Calculation: Semester SGPA Credits SGPA × Credits Sem 1 7.2 27 194.4 Sem 2 8.0 26 208.0 Sem 3 9.0 29 261.0 Sem 4 7.5 27 202.5 Total 109 865.9 CGPA = 865.9 ÷ 109 = 7.94

question: What is the minimum CGPA to graduate?
answer: Undergraduate: Minimum CGPA 5.00 15 Postgraduate: Minimum CGPA 6.00 AND every individual course must have at least Grade C. What this means: You can't compensate for F grades with high grades in other courses. You must clear every course.

question: What is Absolute Grading?
answer: Your grade depends ONLY on your marks, not on others' performance. When used: Batch size ≤ 21 students Characteristics: Fixed grade boundaries announced in advance Transparent rubrics Your performance evaluated independently Predictable outcomes Example: If 85% = O grade, everyone scoring 85%+ gets O, whether 1 person or 10 people. UG Absolute Grading Scale: Marks % Grade Grade Point 85-100 O 10 75-84 A+ 9 65-74 A 8 55-64 B+ 7 45-54 B 6 40-44 C+ 5 35-39 C 4 <35 F 0 PG Absolute Grading Scale: Marks % Grade Grade Point 85-100 O 10 75-84 A+ 9 65-74 A 8 55-64 B+ 7 16 50-54 B 6 45-49 C+ 5 40-44 C 4

question: What is Relative Grading?
answer: Your grade depends on how you perform compared to classmates. When used: Batch size > 21 students Characteristics: Grade boundaries determined by cohort performance Boundaries are confidential (not disclosed) Determined by instructor using academic judgment Students informed only of the approach, not specific cut-offs Example: Top 10% might get O, next 20% get A+, etc. (exact percentages not disclosed).

question: Can I know the grading system for my course in advance?
answer: Absolute Grading: Yes, grade boundaries announced at course start Relative Grading: You'll be told "relative grading applies" but not the specific boundaries

question: Can grading system change mid-semester?
answer: No. Once declared, the grading system (absolute or relative) cannot be changed midsemester. 2.3 TEACHING LEARNING & ASSESSMENT

question: What is the structure of the academic year?
answer: The academic year has: Odd Semester (usually August-December) Even Semester (usually January-May) Summer Semester (optional, usually June-July)

question: What are Course Outcomes (COs)?
answer: Specific learning objectives for each course—what you should know/do after completing it. 17 Example CO: "Students will be able to design and implement database systems using SQL." Why it matters: Your assessment tests whether you've achieved these outcomes.

question: What is the assessment cycle?
answer: Each course has three assessment phases: Continuous Assessment (CA) - Throughout the semester Mid-Course Evaluation (MCE) - Around week 7-8 End-Term Examination - End of semester All three combined determine your final grade.

question: What is a "prerequisite"?
answer: A course you must complete before taking an advanced course. Example: "Data Structures" is prerequisite for "Database Management" You cannot register for DBMS until you've passed Data Structures Key Rules: Prerequisites CANNOT be waived Must show as "passed" (Grade C or higher) Failing a prerequisite blocks registration in dependent courses No exceptions

question: What happens if I haven't cleared a prerequisite?
answer: You cannot register for courses that depend on it. Example scenario: Semester 3: You fail "Data Structures" (DS) Semester 4: You cannot register for "Database Management" (needs DS prerequisite) You must clear DS through Supplementary/Summer/DR/Regular Repeat first 18 2.4 SEMESTER PROGRESSION RULES

question: Can I move to the next semester with failed courses?
answer: Yes, normally. You progress semester-wise subject to: Conditions for progression: Attendance compliance (75% in courses you want to continue) Prerequisite fulfillment (for courses with prerequisites) Programme-specific norms (some programmes have additional rules) PCI/ BCI regulated programs Not in Academic Year Back status due to excessive failures Example: Semester 1: Pass 5 courses, Fail 3 courses Semester 2: You can progress (take Sem 2 courses + work on clearing those 3)

question: Is there a minimum SGPA to progress?
answer: No. You can progress with any SGPA (even 0.00) as long as you meet the conditions in Q2.4.1. However: Low SGPA affects: Academic standing May trigger Academic Year Back if you fail too many credits Affects graduation (need minimum 5.0/6.0 CGPA overall)

question: What are "programme-specific eligibility norms"?
answer: Some programmes (especially those regulated by professional councils) have additional rules. Examples: Pharmacy (PCI-regulated): May have stricter progression rules Law (BCI): May require passing specific core courses Engineering (AICTE): May have lab/practical requirements Where to check: Your Programme Handbook or Programme Coordinator 19

question: What appears on my Grade Card/Transcript?
answer: Your official transcript shows: Per Semester: Courses taken Credits for each course Grades earned SGPA for that semester Cumulative: Total credits earned Overall CGPA Academic standing

question: Do failed attempts appear on my transcript?
answer: Yes. All attempts (Regular, Supplementary, Summer, Directed Reading, etc.) are maintained in the University’s academic records for audit and regulatory purposes.

question: What if I've attempted a course multiple times?
answer: Internal system: Maintains all attempts for University records Official transcript: Shows the best valid attempt or most recent valid grade wherever applicable

question: What is the Academic Bank of Credits (ABC)?
answer: A national digital repository where your credits are stored. Key features: Maintained by UGC Stores all your earned credits Enables credit transfer between institutions Credits valid for 7 years 20 Applicable to credits earned from AY 2021-22 onwards Why it matters: Supports NEP 2020's Multiple Entry-Exit system 21 SECTION 3: ASESSMENT & EVALUATION (AG-02) 22 3.1 ASSESSMENT PHILOSOPHY

question: What is the purpose of assessment at UPES?
answer: Assessment serves dual purposes: 1. Formative (Learning): Provide ongoing feedback Help you improve Guide your learning journey 2. Summative (Certification): Measure final achievement Award grades Certify competency UPES emphasizes continuous/formative assessment (70%) over final exam (30%).

question: What does "instructor-led, outcome-oriented" mean?
answer: Instructor-led: Your faculty designs assessments aligned with course outcomes Outcome-oriented: Assessments test whether you've achieved Course Outcomes (COs) Not arbitrary: Every assessment ties to specific learning goals

question: Why 70% continuous assessment and only 30% End-Term?
answer: This structure: Reduces over-dependence on single final exam Evaluates learning progressively Measures both knowledge and application Provides multiple opportunities to demonstrate competency Aligns with modern pedagogy (NEP 2020) Philosophy: Your semester-long work matters more than one 2-hour exam. 23 3.2 Standard Assessment Structure

question: I enrolled in 2021-22 or 2022-23. Does the same structure apply to me?
answer: No. You follow the structure applicable at your enrollment: For 2021-22 and 2022-23 batches: Component Weightage Internal Assessment 30% Mid-Semester Examination 20% End-Term Examination 50% TOTAL 100% Why different: Ensures continuity for earlier batches; new students get revised structure.

question: Does this apply to ALL courses?
answer: This is the standard for theory courses.

question: Which courses might have 100% CA (no End-Term)?
answer: Typically courses where continuous evaluation is more appropriate: Examples: Studio-based courses (Architecture, Design) Laboratory-intensive courses Project-centric courses 24 Practice-driven courses (Clinical, Field work) Skill -based courses (Programming labs, Workshops) Requirements for 100% CA: Academically defensible (justified by course nature) Outcome-aligned (tests COs effectively) Clearly documented (in syllabus) Academic Council pre-approval (not retroactive)

question: How will I know if my course has 100% CA?
answer: Must be explicitly stated in: Course syllabus Communicated at course start (first week) Uniform across all sections Important: Faculty cannot decide mid-semester to drop End-Term Exam.

question: Does 100% CA mean easier/less rigorous?
answer: No. 100% CA means: More frequent assessments Ongoing evaluation Often MORE work, not less Different format, same rigor Example: A design studio might have weekly critiques, mid-term jury, final jury, portfolio—all counted in CA. 3.3 CONTINUOUS ASSESSMENT (CA) DETAILS

question: What exactly is Continuous Assessment (CA)?
answer: All evaluation conducted during the semester, before End-Term Exam. CA = Internal Assessments (50%) + Mid-Course Evaluation (20%) 25

question: What can be included in Internal Assessments (50%)?
answer: Depending on course design, any of these: Assessment Type Examples Assignments Written, problem sets, case studies Quizzes Announced or surprise Class Tests In-class written tests Projects Individual or group Presentations Individual or group Lab Work Practical assessments, experiments Term Papers Research papers, essays Open-Book Exams Take-home or in-class Class Participation Discussions, Q&A, engagement Others Any academically approved task Key: Faculty decides the mix based on Course Outcomes.

question: How many CA components will there be?
answer: Varies by course. Your instructor must disclose: Number of assessments Type of each assessment Individual weightages Mode of evaluation Disclosure timeline: On or before the first day of class, published on LMS.

question: Can CA components/weightages change mid-semester?
answer: No. Normal rule: What's announced on Day 1 stays. 26

question: What if my CA marks are very low—can I still pass?
answer: CA contributes 70%, so low CA makes passing very difficult. Scenarios: If CA > 0 and attendance ≥75%: You can appear for End-Term and Supplementary if needed If CA = 0: You cannot take Supplementary (must repeat full course) Strategy: Take CA seriously—it's the majority of your grade. 3.4 MID-COURSE EVALUATION (MCE)

question: What is Mid-Course Evaluation (MCE)?
answer: A mandatory formal exam conducted around the semester midpoint (usually Week 7-8). Key characteristics: 2-hour duration Tests first half of course content Carries 20% weightage Part of Continuous Assessment (CA) Marks contribute to final grade

question: Is MCE the same as "Mid-Semester Exam"?
answer: Yes it is new name for Mid-semester exam.

question: Is MCE mandatory to attend?
answer: Yes. It's a formal examination component. If you miss MCE: You get zero for that 20% Significantly hurts your final grade May affect Supplementary eligibility (if CA becomes too low)

question: Can I get re-evaluation for MCE?
answer: No. Re-evaluation is only permitted for End-Term Examination. For MCE/CA issues: Use the appeals process for procedural/computational errors only (Section 18). 27 3.5 END-TERM EXAMINATION

question: What is the End-Term Examination?
answer: The final comprehensive exam at semester end, covering cumulative course learning outcomes. Key features: Usually 2-3 hours duration Covers entire course or major portions Carries 30% weightage (standard structure) Conducted in controlled conditions Scheduled by Controller of Examinations (CoE)

question: Is appearing for End-Term Exam mandatory?
answer: YES, ABSOLUTELY MANDATORY. Critical Rule: "A student who does not appear for the End-Term Examination shall be declared failed in the course, irrespective of marks obtained in Continuous Assessment." This means: Even if you scored 70/70 in CA Even if you have an A+ level performance If you don't appear for ETE, you get F (Fail)

question: What is "debarment"?
answer: Being barred/blocked from appearing for End-Term Examination. Common reason: Attendance below 75% Consequence: You cannot sit for the End Term Exam You get 'Ab' (Absent) grade Cannot take Supplementary Exam Must clear through Summer/DR/Regular Repeat which may have additional financial impact

question: Does the grade calculation change if I took Supplementary?
answer: Calculation method stays the same: CA marks carried forward (no re-earning) Supplementary replaces End Term Examination component Final = (Old CA × 0.70) + (Supplementary ETE × 0.30) Example: 29 Regular attempt: CA 40, ETE 10 → Total 50 (Fail) Supplementary: CA 40 (same), ETE 25 → Total 65 (Pass, Grade A)

question: How does my course grade affect SGPA?
answer: Each course grade is converted to grade points, multiplied by credits, then used in SGPA calculation. Formula (repeated for clarity): SGPA = Σ(Grade Point × Credits) ÷ Total Credits Course grades awarded under this policy feed directly into SGPA/CGPA. 3.7 SUPPLEMENTARY ASSESSMENT RULES

question: What is Supplementary Assessment?
answer: If you fail a course and take Supplementary Examination, your assessment follows specific rules. Assessment structure: Weightage = End-Term component only (30%) CA marks carried forward (70%) No grade capping: You can get any grade (O to F) in Supplementary.

question: Are there any restrictions on Supplementary grades?
answer: No grade capping as per this policy. Meaning: If you score 90+ in Supplementary, you can get Grade O No penalty for taking Supplementary vs. regular attempt However, the grade card and transcript shall reflect second attempt with an astirx*

question: What if I fail the Supplementary too?
answer: Move to next recovery pathway: Summer Semester Directed Reading Regular Repeat Supplementary Exam Subject to maximum of 3 attempts 30 3.8 ACADEMIC INTEGRITY IN ASSESSMENTS

question: What is academic misconduct?
answer: Any form of cheating or unfair means in assessments: Plagiarism Copying in exams Unauthorized collaboration Use of prohibited materials Impersonation Governed by: UPES Academic Integrity & Student Academic Misconduct Policy

question: What happens if I'm caught cheating?
answer: Penalties as per Academic Integrity Policy, which may include: Zero in that assessment F grade in course Disciplinary action Suspension/expulsion (severe cases) This is separate from the academic failure recovery process—misconduct has additional consequences.

question: Can I use AI tools (ChatGPT, etc.) for assignments?
answer: Depends on course policy: Some courses may allow with citation Some may prohibit entirely Check with your instructor for each course General principle: If you use AI, you must: Disclose it Follow instructor guidelines Not present AI work as your own Undisclosed AI use = Plagiarism 31

question: Can this Assessment Policy change?
answer: Yes, policies are reviewed periodically. Process: Academic Council reviews Amendments approved through governance Changes communicated officially Typically: Changes apply to new batches; current students grandfathered under old rules.

question: What is the attendance philosophy at UPES?
answer: Attendance is an academic eligibility requirement, not a disciplinary measure. Three-tier structure: 100% expected: You should attend everything 25% flexibility: Automatic buffer for unforeseen circumstances 75% mandatory: Minimum threshold for End-Term eligibility

question: What is the minimum attendance required?
answer: 75% attendance in each course is mandatory. Key clarifications: Course-wise: Each course calculated separately (not overall) Strict threshold: 75.0% or above (no rounding) Eligibility: To appear for End-Term Examination

question: Why is 75% attendance mandatory?
answer: UGC/regulatory requirement Ensures classroom engagement Necessary for learning outcomes Industry/professional expectation

question: Does UPES expect 100% attendance?
answer: Yes, 100% is the expectation. But: 25% flexibility built in (you can miss up to 25% without consequences) This 25% covers illness, emergencies, personal situations The 75% minimum is the eligibility floor Think of it as: Target: 100% Acceptable: 75-100% Ineligible: Below 75% 34 4.2 ATTENDANCE CALCULATION & TRACKING

question: How is attendance calculated?
answer: Attendance % = (Classes Attended ÷ Total Classes Conducted) × 100 Calculated separately for each course. Example: Total classes conducted: 40 You attended: 32 Attendance = 32/40 × 100 = 80%

question: Is attendance calculated course-wise or overall?
answer: Course-wise (per course, not programme-wide). Example scenario: Course Attendance Status Mathematics 90% Eligible Physics 78% Eligible Programming 72% Debarred English 85% Eligible You're debarred from Programming End Term Exam, but eligible for others.

question: What counts as a "class" for attendance?
answer: All scheduled academic activities: Lectures Tutorials Laboratory sessions Studio sessions Field work Practical sessions Project guidance sessions (if scheduled) If it's on your timetable, it counts. 35

question: How is attendance recorded?
answer: Through University-approved systems: ERP LMS-integrated mechanisms Official system record is final and binding.

question: What if there's an error in attendance recording?
answer: Check ERP regularly (don't wait until end of semester) Report errors immediately to faculty If unresolved, contact APO Office/ Deans Office Provide proof if available (emails, documentation) Important: Report errors promptly—don't wait until you're at 74%.

question: Can I dispute attendance records?
answer: Only for data/computational errors: System glitches Faculty marking mistakes All of above with proofs Not valid disputes: "I don't remember missing that many classes" "I was there but didn't mark biometric" "I listened to recording later" Process: Appeal through Academic Appeals process (Section 18), limited to factual errors. 4.3 THE 25% FLEXIBILITY BUFFER

question: What is the 25% flexibility?
answer: An automatic allowance for up to 25% absence without needing approval or documentation. Example: Course has 40 classes 25% of 40 = 10 classes You can miss up to 10 classes without any questions asked 36 As long as you attend 30/40 (75%), you're eligible

question: What does this 25% cover?
answer: ANY reason: All kinds of Illness Personal emergencies Family situations Festivals/religious observances Interviews, competitive exams Representing University of different forums Anything No documentation required. No approval needed. No questions asked.

question: What if I have a documented medical emergency that causes >25% absence?
answer: The policy provides the 25% buffer, but: No explicit provision for extended medical absence Recommendation: Contact Dean's Office if you have prolonged illness 4.4 THE 75% MANDATORY THRESHOLD

question: What exactly happens at 75% attendance?
answer: 75% is the minimum eligibility to appear for End-Term Examination (ETE ). If attendance ≥ 75%: Eligible for ETE 37 Can complete course normally If attendance < 75%: Debarred from ETE Get 'Ab' (Absent) grade Must use recovery pathways excluding supplementary examinations

question: I have exactly 75.0% attendance. Am I eligible?
answer: Yes. 75.0% meets the threshold. The rule: "Minimum 75%" means ≥ 75.0%.

question: I have 74.9% attendance. Can it be rounded to 75%?
answer: NO. Absolutely not. Policy explicitly states: "No rounding up, approximation, or discretionary adjustment shall be permitted." 74.9% = Below 75% = Debarment. No exceptions.

question: I have 74.8% but performed excellently in CA. Can that compensate?
answer: No. High academic performance does NOT compensate for attendance shortage. The policy is clear: Attendance is an eligibility requirement Not an academic performance metric Cannot be offset by grades Even if you have perfect CA scores, 74% attendance = Debarment.

question: Why is the University so strict about 74% vs. 75%?
answer: Regulatory requirement (UGC/statutory councils) Clear line prevents subjective decisions Fairness (same rule for everyone) 25% buffer already generous You had 25% flexibility; staying above 75% is your responsibility. 38 4.5 DEBARMENT (ATTENDANCE SHORTAGE)

question: What is debarment?
answer: Being barred/blocked from appearing for the End-Term Examination due to attendance shortage. Cause: Attendance < 75% in a course.

question: What happens if I'm debarred?
answer: For that specific course: You cannot sit for the End-Term Exam You receive 'Ab' grade (Absent due to attendance shortage) This counts as F (Fail) academically You cannot take Supplementary Examination You must repeat the course through: Summer Semester, OR Directed Reading (DR), OR Regular Course Repeat (with next batch)

question: Can debarment be appealed or waived?
answer: No Policy states: "Under no circumstances shall attendance relaxation be granted."

question: What if I'm debarred in multiple courses?
answer: Each debarment is handled separately: You fail all debarred courses Must recover each through Summer/DR/Repeat May trigger Academic Year Back if more than 50% credit failures (Section 6) This can seriously impact your progression. 39 4.6 ATTENDANCE & ACADEMIC RECOVERY

question: I'm debarred (attendance shortage). Can I take Supplementary Exam?
answer: No. Supplementary is only for students who: Had ≥75% attendance, AND Failed academically (F grade) but have adequate marks in IA and ETE to pass the course Debarred students are NOT eligible for Supplementary.

question: What are my options after debarment?
answer: Three pathways: 1. Summer Semester: Full course repeat in summer Attendance + CA + ETE (all fresh) Fee applies (₹3,000/credit) Registration in 2 courses permitted 2. Directed Reading (DR): Faculty-supervised independent study Limited slots Fee applies (₹3,000/credit) Subject to School approval Registration in maximum of 15% of total credits in a semester 3. Regular Course Repeat: Full course repeat with next batch No extra fee (part of regular semester fee)* Must fit in your timetable All detailed in Sections 7-10. *Regular Repeat beyond N duration will incur extension fees.

question: Does debarment count as a "recovery attempt"?
answer: Yes. When you clear a debarred course through Summer/DR/Repeat, it counts as one recovery attempt. You have maximum 2 recovery attempts per course (Section 5). 40

question: What about Summer Semester attendance?
answer: Same 75% rule applies to Summer courses.

question: What about Directed Reading attendance?
answer: DR has "minimum 75% engagement" requirement: Must attend scheduled faculty meetings 1 contact hour per week mandatory Failure to maintain engagement = Fail DR Details in Section 8.

question: What about Regular Course Repeat attendance?
answer: When you repeat a course: Fresh attendance starts from zero 41 Must achieve ≥75% in the repeat attempt No carry-forward of previous attendance You're essentially a new student in that course.

question: Can attendance issues trigger Academic Year Back?
answer: Yes. How: Low attendance → Debarment → 'Ab' grades → Failures If you fail ≥50% annual credits → Academic Year Back (Section 6) Example: You're debarred in 4 courses (attendance shortage) Those 4 courses = 16 credits failed If your annual load is 40 credits, 16 is 40% (not yet AYB) But if you also fail 2 more courses academically, total = 24 credits (60%) → AYB So attendance + academic struggles = High risk of AYB.

question: Does Academic Year Back reset my attendance?
answer: Attendance is course-specific. If you're in AYB: You must re-register for failed courses Attendance for those courses starts fresh Must achieve ≥75% in the repeat attempt AYB is about credit recovery, not attendance waiver. 42 SECTION 5: ACADEMIC RECOVERY PATHWAYS (AG-04) 43 5.1 UNDERSTANDING ACADEMIC RECOVERY

question: What are Academic Recovery Pathways (ARP)?
answer: Official University mechanisms that allow you to clear failed or debarred courses. The six approved pathways: Supplementary Examination Summer Semester Directed Reading (DR) Regular Course Repeat Course Substitution (electives only) Academic Year Back (mandatory for excessive failure) Detailed individually in Sections 6-11.

question: What does "more rigorous than regular" mean?
answer: Policy states: "All Academic Recovery Pathways shall be academically more rigorous than regular course attempts." In practice: Higher academic discipline expected Stricter eligibility conditions More scrutiny on performance Limited attempts (can't keep repeating forever) Example: Summer Semester is intensive (compressed timeline, full workload).

question: Are recovery pathways easier than regular courses?
answer: NO. They're actually harder: Challenges: Summer Semester: Condensed timeline (6 weeks vs. 16 weeks) Directed Reading: Self-directed, requires discipline Regular Repeat: Managing two cohorts of courses simultaneously Academic Year Back: Heavy backlog load + new courses Think of it as: Second chance, not easy chance. 5.2 ATTEMPT LIMITS

question: How many times can I attempt a course?
answer: 1 Regular Attempt (first time taking the course) Up to 2 Recovery Attempts (through any ARP combination) Mandatory Regular Repeat (if recovery attempts exhausted) Total: 1 regular + 2 recovery + mandatory repeats if needed.

question: What counts as a "recovery attempt"?
answer: Any attempt through Academic Recovery Pathways: Supplementary Examination Summer Semester Directed Reading 45 Regular Course Repeat Course Substitution also counts as one attempt. Basis the eligibility to appear

question: Can I strategically use my recovery attempts?
answer: Example scenarios: Scenario 1: Regular attempt: F (Attendance >= 75%) Recovery 1: Supplementary → F Recovery 2: Summer Semester/ Supplementary → Pass Scenario 2: Regular attempt: Ab (debarred) Recovery 1: Summer Semester → F Recovery 2: Directed Reading/ Summer semester → Pass After 2 recovery attempts exhausted: Must take Regular Course Repeat (mandatory, full course)

question: What if I exhaust my 2 recovery attempts and still fail?
answer: You must take Regular Course Repeat: Full course repeat with next batch Fresh CA + MCE + ETE No carry-forward of any components This is mandatory, not optional Continues until you pass (within N+2+1 duration).

question: Can I take unlimited Supplementary Exams?
answer: Yes. Supplementary is one recovery attempt, however student can use other recovery pathways as well (Summer/DR/Repeat) limited to 3 attempts including the original attempt. Think of it as: Supplementary is your "quick fix" chance—use it wisely. 46 5.3 ELIGIBILITY FOR RECOVERY PATHWAYS

question: Why can't debarred students take Supplementary?
answer: Your Learning was not complete since you did not attend classes as required

question: Can I choose any recovery pathway I want?
answer: Not always. Limited by: Eligibility Course availability (not all courses offered in Summer/DR) Seat capacity Faculty availability School approval You have choices, but not unlimited choices. 5.4 ACADEMIC YEAR BACK (AYB) AS RECOVERY MECHANISM

question: What is Academic Year Back?
answer: A mandatory structured recovery year when you fail ≥50% of annual credits. Trigger: 47 Failed Credits ÷ Annual Registered Credits ≥ 50% Example: Annual load: 40 credits (Odd + Even semester) You fail: 22 credits Percentage: 22/40 = 55% → AYB triggered

question: Is Academic Year Back a punishment?
answer: No, it's a recovery and progression control mechanism. What it does: Forces you to prioritize clearing backlogs Limits new course registration (max 50% of normal load) Provides structured recovery plan (Individual Study Plan) Prevents uncontrolled backlog accumulation Philosophy: Better to take a year to recover than keep accumulating failures.

question: Can I avoid Academic Year Back by working hard in recovery?
answer: AYB is triggered retrospectively based on past academic year performance. Once triggered: It's mandatory (not discretionary) You cannot avoid it by promising to do better Must follow AYB rules for recovery Prevention: Don't let failures accumulate to 50% in the first place. 5.5 RECOVERY PATHWAY COSTS

question: Can fees be waived for financial hardship?
answer: No fee waivers for recovery pathways.

question: What if I can't afford Summer Semester or DR?
answer: Your option is Regular Course Repeat: No extra fee (within normal duration) Repeat with next batch Must manage timetable clashes yourself Trade-off: No extra fee, but: Delay in progression Timetable management challenges Longer time to graduation 5.6 RECOVERY & PROGRAMME DURATION

question: Do recovery attempts count toward N+2+1?
answer: Yes, everything counts: Summer Semesters Academic Year Back periods Regular Repeat attempts All count toward maximum programme duration No "free time" for recovery.

question: What if I can't clear backlogs within N+2+1?
answer: You cannot continue the programme. Options: 49 Exit with earned credits (Certificate/Diploma (if eligible) under NEP 2020 Discontinue without degree This is why recovery planning is critical. 5.7 RECOVERY & PREREQUISITES

question: If I fail a prerequisite course, what happens?
answer: You're blocked from registering for courses that depend on it. Example: Fail "Calculus I" (prerequisite for "Calculus II") Cannot register for Calculus II until you pass Calculus I Progression impact: Can create a bottleneck in your academic journey.

question: Can prerequisites be waived for recovery?
answer: NO. Never. Policy is absolute: "Prerequisites shall not be waived under any circumstances." Even if: You're graduating late You promise to work hard You scored high in related courses Prerequisites are non-negotiable.

question: How do I handle prerequisite blocks?
answer: Prioritize clearing prerequisite courses first: Strategy: Identify which failed courses are prerequisites for future courses Clear those first (Supplementary/Summer/DR/Regular Repeat) Then clear other backlogs Example: Failed: Data Structures (prerequisite for 3 advanced courses) + English (no prerequisite for anything) Priority: Clear Data Structures first (unblocks 3 courses) English can be cleared later 50

question: What's the credit limit including recovery courses?
answer: Students may register for up to 27 credits in a regular semester. For BCI/PCI‑regulated programmes, where the prescribed semester load exceeds 24 credits, an additional credit overload of up to 15%of the semester’s credit requirement may be permitted. Summer Semester: Additional 2 courses (not counted toward 27) DR: Limited to 15% of semester credits (separate calculation) Q5.8.3: Should I overload to clear backlogs faster? A: Not recommended. High risk of: Burnout Poor performance in all courses Creating more backlogs 51 Better strategy: Balanced load, focused effort. Example: Regular semeste

question: Do recovery attempts affect my transcript?
answer: Yes. All attempts (Regular, Supplementary, Summer, Directed Reading, etc.) are maintained in the University’s academic records for audit and regulatory purposes. 5.9 CHOOSING THE RIGHT RECOVERY PATHWAY

question: Example decision scenarios Scenario
answer: Failed "Marketing Management" (elective) Attendance was 80%, failed ETE 52 Not a prerequisite for anything Recommendation: Supplementary Exam Quickest (few weeks) Only need to retake ETE Scenario B: Failed "Data Structures" (core course) Attendance was 72% (debarred) Prerequisite for 3 future courses Recommendation: Summer Semester (prioritize over other backlogs) Can't do Supplementary (debarred) Prerequisite block is critical Summer clears it before next semester Registration in only 2 courses is allowed Scenario C: Failed "Advanced Calculus" (elective) Attendance OK, failed academically Alr

question: Can I consult someone before deciding?
answer: Yes, absolutely recommended: Consult: Program Leader/ Cluster Head: Understands your programme structure, prerequisites Academic Mentor: Knows your academic history, can advise on feasibility Deans Office: Can clarify policy, timelines, procedures, get you help from faculty experts Don't decide in isolation—get expert guidance. 5.10 RECOVERY PATHWAY GOVERNANCE

question: Who approves my recovery pathway?
answer: Depends on pathway: Pathway Approval Authority Process Supplementary Auto-approved if eligible Online registration via ERP Summer Semester APO + School Registration process Directed Reading APO+ School Dean + Faculty availability Application + approval Regular Repeat Auto-approved Normal course registration- Time Table Clash should not be there Course Substitution Dean of School Formal application Academic Year Back Auto-triggered Individual Study Plan by Programme Lead

question: What if I'm denied a recovery pathway?
answer: Explore alternative pathways Can appeal through Academic Appeals process, if you believe denial was procedural error

question: Can I change my recovery pathway mid-process?
answer: No. Once the Registration process is complete it will be considered as an attempt Example: Registered for Summer Semester 54 Registered and Started classes, realize it's too hard Cannot switch to DR mid-Summer Must complete or fail Choose carefully before committing. 5.11 MULTIPLE FAILURES & STRATEGIC PLANNING

question: I have multiple backlogs. How do I prioritize?
answer: Prioritization matrix: Priority 1 (Clear First): Prerequisite courses blocking future courses Courses triggering Academic Year Back (if backlog >=50%) Core/mandatory courses Priority 2 (Clear Next): Courses offered infrequently Courses with limited recovery options Courses close to attempt limit Priority 3 (Can defer): Electives (substitution possible) Courses with multiple recovery options available

question: Can I clear all backlogs in one Summer Semester?
answer: No, maximum 2 courses per Summer Semester. If you have 5 backlogs: Summer Year 1: Clear 2 Supplementary: Maybe 1-2 more Summer Year 2: Remaining OR mix with DR, Regular Repeat Realistically: 3-4 backlogs take 1-2 years to clear. 55

question: What if I keep failing recovery attempts?
answer: After exhausting 2 recovery attempts per course: Must do Regular Course Repeat This continues until you pass OR exceed N+2+1 If pattern of repeated failures: May indicate deeper issues (study skills, personal problems, wrong programme) Strongly recommend: Academic counselling Reconsider programme fit Consider Change of Programme/Major (if more suitable option exists) Persistent failure = Risk of not graduating. Take seriously. 56 SECTION 6: ACADEMIC YEAR BACK & PROGRESSION POLICY(AG-05) 57 6.1 UNDERSTANDING ACADEMIC YEAR BACK

question: What is Academic Year Back (AYB)?
answer: A mandatory structured recovery-and-progression year triggered when you fail a large portion of your annual coursework. Key characteristics: Automatic: Not optional or discretionary Mandatory backlog clearance: Must register for all failed courses first Limited forward progression: Can take some new courses (max 50% of normal load depending on the % of credits yet to be cleared) Individual Study Plan: Customized plan created for you Applicable from admission year 2025

question: Why does AYB exist?
answer: To prevent uncontrolled backlog accumulation that leads to: Impossible graduation timelines Overwhelming academic load Violation of prerequisite chains Exceeding maximum programme duration (N+2+1) Philosophy: Better to pause, consolidate, and recover than keep spiralling.

question: Is AYB a punishment?
answer: No, it's a protective mechanism. Think of it as: Circuit breaker (stops the problem from worsening) Recovery framework (structured approach to catch up) Progression control (prevents prerequisite violations) Not punitive, but serious.

question: Does AYB mean I repeat the entire year?
answer: No. What AYB does NOT do: Invalidate or nullify earned credits 58 Force you to repeat passed courses Reset your academic record What AYB DOES do: Requires mandatory registration in all failed courses Limits new course registration Provides structured recovery plan You keep all passed credits—you're only recovering failed ones. 6.2 AYB TRIGGER CONDITIONS

question: When is AYB triggered?
answer: When you fail 50% or more of total registered credits in an academic year. Formula: AYB Triggered if: (Failed Credits ÷ Credits due to be earned) ≥ 50% Academic year = Odd Semester + Even Semester combined.

question: How is "failed credits" calculated?
answer: Includes: Courses with F grade (academic failure) Courses with Ab grade (attendance shortage) Any course not cleared (not passed)

question: What if I fail exactly 50%—is AYB triggered?
answer: Yes. The policy states "50% or more." 50% = AYB triggered.

question: Does AYB consider attendance-based failures (Ab grade)?
answer: Yes. 'Ab' grades are counted as failed credits for AYB calculation. Example: Annual: 40 credits 10 credits: F (academic failure) 12 credits: Ab (attendance shortage) 60 Total failed: 22 credits (55%) → AYB triggered 6.3 AYB NOTIFICATION & PROCESS

question: How will I know if I'm placed on AYB?
answer: Official communication: Email notification from the university ERP system alert May require meeting with Programme Lead/ Cluster Head Timeline: Usually after Even Semester results are declared (end of academic year).

question: Can I appeal against AYB placement?
answer: No, because AYB is: Rule-based (objective 50% threshold) Automatic (not discretionary) Non-negotiable Not valid grounds: "I didn't know the rule" "I had personal problems" "Please give me one more chance" If you believe there's an error: Contact APO immediately with documentation. 6.4 AYB REGISTRATION FRAMEWORK

question: Can I skip registering for a failed course?
answer: No. All failed courses MUST be registered for. Important: Course substitution (if elective—requires approval) Course no longer offered (requires equivalence mapping) Otherwise: Mandatory registration. 62

question: Can I take more than 27 credits in a semester during AYB?
answer: No, the 27-credit semester limit still applies. So within AYB year: You have a certain total annual capacity (backlogs + new) But semester-wise, max 27 credits This may require strategic planning across Odd and Even semesters. Example: Total AYB load: 40 credits (20 backlogs + 20 new) Distribution: Odd Semester: 20 credits (mix of backlogs + new) Even Semester: 20 credits (mix of backlogs + new) 63 Both within 27-credit limit ** For BCI/PCI‑regulated programmes, where the prescribed semester load exceeds 24 credits, an additional credit overload of up to 15%of the semester’s credit requirement

question: What if my backlogs alone exceed 27 credits in one semester?
answer: This is a serious problem. Scenarios: You failed 30 credits in Odd Semester Next Odd Semester: Can only register max 27 credits Remaining 3 credits become a problem Solutions (require Programme Coordinator guidance): Use Summer Semester to clear some backlogs before AYB year Use DR for some courses Extend beyond one AYB year (impacts graduation timeline) This is why AYB exists—to prevent such situations. 6.5 INDIVIDUAL STUDY PLAN (ISP)

question: What is an Individual Study Plan (ISP)?
answer: A customized academic plan created for every AYB student, detailing: Which backlog courses to clear each semester Which new courses (if any) you can take Semester-wise credit load Expected graduation timeline Impact on overall programme duration Created by: Programme Lead / Cluster Head in consultation with you.

question: Is ISP mandatory?
answer: Yes, absolutely. Process: You're notified of AYB placement Mandatory meeting with Programme Lead / Cluster Head ISP created based on: Your backlogs (which semesters, prerequisites) 64 Credit limits Course availability Graduation feasibility ISP signed by: Programme Lead / Cluster Head You (the student) Submitted to APO Your registration is controlled manually per ISP (not open registration)

question: Can I deviate from my ISP?
answer: No, not without Dean-level approval. ISP is binding: You must follow the plan Deviations require formal approval Unapproved changes may block registration If circumstances change (new timetable clash, course not offered): Immediately contact Programme Lead Request ISP revision Get written approval

question: What happens during ISP advising meeting?
answer: The Programme Lead/Cluster Head will: Review your failures: Which courses failed Why (attendance vs. academic) Prerequisites blocked Assess recovery options: Which backlogs through Supplementary/Summer/DR Which through regular repeat Plan progression: Semester-wise registration Prerequisite sequencing Credit load management Discuss implications: Graduation delay 65 Financial impact (Summer/DR fees, extension fees) Maximum duration compliance Provide guidance: Study strategies Time management Resource utilization (tutoring, counseling) This is a collaborative planning session, not a scolding.

question: Can my ISP include Summer Semester or DR?
answer: Yes, ISP can and should incorporate all recovery options: Example ISP Note: "Student is advised to take Supplementary Examination for Physics-I and Programming. If either Supplementary fails, student must enroll in Summer Semester for that course before Odd Semester Year 2." ISP serves as: Primary plan (main registration) Contingency plan (if Supplementary fails, do Summer) Comprehensive roadmap 6.6 ACADEMIC PROGRESSION POST-AYB

question: What happens after completing my AYB year?
answer: If you've cleared most/all backlogs: You exit AYB status Return to normal progression Restrictions lifted (can take full course loads) If you still have backlogs: May continue in modified AYB or normal progression (depends on extent) Must still prioritize backlog clearance Still subject to N+2+1 duration limits

question: Can I have AYB multiple times?
answer: Technically yes, if you trigger 50% failure again. However: Second AYB = Very serious situation 66 Indicates pattern of difficulty Practically: Second AYB is rare and problematic.

question: Does AYB delay my graduation?
answer: Usually yes, by at least one year. Typical scenarios: Normal 4-year programme AYB in Year 1-2 Actual graduation: Year 5 (still within N+2) However: If you strategically use Summer/DR and clear backlogs quickly, might graduate in original timeline. 6.7 AYB & FINANCIAL IMPACT

question: What are "extension fees"?
answer: Fees charged when you continue beyond normal programme duration (N years). Example: 4-year BTech (N=4) AYB causes completion in Year 5 Year 5 is "extension" (within N+2, but beyond N) Extension fees apply for Year 5 semesters Amount: As notified by University (could be full semester fees or partial). 6.9 AYB, PREREQUISITES & ACADEMIC STANDING

question: Do prerequisite rules still apply during AYB?
answer: Yes, strictly enforced. 67 Even in AYB: Cannot register for advanced course without clearing prerequisite Backlog clearance may be sequenced to handle prerequisites first

question: Does AYB reset my academic year numbering?
answer: No. You remain in your admitted batch. Example: Admitted: 2025 batch AYB in 2026-27 academic year You're still "2025 batch" student Not moved to 2026 batch Implications: Transcript shows your original admission year You may attend classes with junior batches (they're in Year 1, you're repeating Year 1 courses) But you're still senior batch student 68 SECTION 7: SUPPLEMENTARY EXAMINATION POLICY (AG-06) 69 7.1 UNDERSTANDING SUPPLEMENTARY EXAMS

question: What is a Supplementary Examination?
answer: A re-examination opportunity where you retake only the End-Term Examination component (30%) to clear a failed course. Key features: Only End-Term re-evaluated (not CA or MCE) CA marks carried forward from original attempt No grade capping (can get any grade from C to O)

question: When is Supplementary used?
answer: When you: Failed a course due to poor End-Term performance Met attendance requirement (≥75%) Have CA > 0 and CA is sufficient to mathematically pass if you do well in End-Term

question: What's the difference between Supplementary Exam and Summer Semester?
answer: Feature Supplementary Exam Summer Semester Duration Single exam (few hours) Full course (6 weeks) Components End-Term only Full CA + MCE + ETE Attendance Not applicable (just exam) 75% mandatory CA marks Carried forward Fresh evaluation Fee ₹2,000 per course ₹3,000 per credit Timeline Within weeks of results Summer break Eligibility ≥75% attendance Any failure type

question: Can Supplementary be used for grade improvement?
answer: Yes, but subject to separate Grade Improvement Policy (Section 12). 70 Two uses: Clearing F grade: Main use, covered in this section Improving passed grade: Governed by AG-11 (Grade Improvement Policy) Details on improvement in Section 12. 7.2 SUPPLEMENTARY ELIGIBILITY (CLEARING F GRADE)

question: Is there grade capping in Supplementary?
answer: No grade capping. Meaning: You can score high in Supplementary

question: What if I fail the Supplementary Exam?
answer: Move to next recovery pathway: Summer Semester Directed Reading Regular Course Repeat Supplementary used one of your two recovery attempts. 7.4 SUPPLEMENTARY EXAM CYCLES & FEES

question: When does registration open?
answer: After result declaration, window typically 7-15 days.

question: What is the Supplementary Exam fee?
answer: ₹2,000 per course. Payment: Online (ERP portal) Mandatory at registration time Non-refundable

question: Is the fee refundable if I don't appear?
answer: No. Policy states: "Refund / Withdrawal: Not permitted" Once paid and registered: Fee is non-refundable Even if you change your mind Even if you don't appear for the exam

question: What if I'm registered but later found ineligible?
answer: Depends on who made the error: If University error (system bug, wrong eligibility determination): Fee may be refunded Case-by-case basis If student error (you incorrectly thought you were eligible): Fee NOT refundable Attempt still counted This is why checking eligibility carefully is critical. 74

question: Can I withdraw my Supplementary registration?
answer: No. Once registered: Cannot withdraw Cannot get refund If you don't appear → Counted as attempt used, result = Absent (F) Think before you register.

question: Can I apply for re-evaluation of Supplementary Exam?
answer: Yes. Same re-evaluation rules apply: Can apply for re-evaluation 7.5 SUPPLEMENTARY FOR GRADE IMPROVEMENT

question: Can I use Supplementary to improve a passing grade?
answer: Yes, subject to Grade Improvement Policy (AG-11). Conditions (brief overview—details in Section 12): You already passed the course (Grade C or higher) Want to improve to a higher grade Within Grade Improvement attempt limits (1 attempt per course) Process: Apply for Supplementary under "Improvement" category Pay fee Appear for exam Outcome: New grade (if higher) replaces old grade If lower, old grade stands (usually) Full details in Section 12. 75

question: What is Directed Reading (DR)?
answer: A faculty-supervised independent study pathway where you complete a failed course through: Self-directed learning (not classroom-based) Weekly faculty consultations (mandatory) Structured learning plan Formal assessment (CA + ETE) Think of it as: Personalized tutorial mode for course recovery.

question: When should I consider DR?
answer: DR is suitable when: Course not offered in Summer Semester You prefer independent study over classroom You have strong self-discipline Faculty is available to supervise Timetable clash makes Regular Repeat difficult

question: Is DR easier than regular courses?
answer: No, DR is actually MORE challenging. Why: Self-directed: You manage your own learning Rigor: Assessment standards are higher (as per policy: "more rigorous") Discipline: Requires strong time management and motivation Isolation: No peer learning, limited interaction DR is for serious, disciplined students, not a shortcut. 8.2 DR ELIGIBILITY

question: Which courses are NOT eligible for DR?
answer: DR is not permitted for: Pure laboratories Studio courses (architecture, design) Workshop courses Internships Dissertations / Capstone projects Practice-based courses (clinical, field work) Reason: These require hands-on, supervised, real-time engagement—can't be self-directed. 79 8.3 DR LIMITS & RESTRICTIONS

question: Are DR assessments easier than regular courses?
answer: No, often harder. Policy explicitly states: "Assessments should be more rigorous than the regular course" Why: You're recovering from failure—higher accountability Faculty may set higher standards Self-study component means you need to demonstrate mastery Don't expect leniency—expect rigor. 8.6 DR ATTENDANCE & ENGAGEMENT

question: What is the fee for Directed Reading?
answer: ₹3,000 per credit. 82 Calculation: If course is 4 credits: ₹3,000 × 4 = ₹12,000 If course is 3 credits: ₹3,000 × 3 = ₹9,000

question: When do I pay the DR fee?
answer: At the time of registration (after DR approval). Process: You're notified via email/ERP Pay fee online through Student Portal Registration confirmed only after fee payment

question: Is the DR fee refundable?
answer: Only in specific cases: Refundable: University cancels DR (faculty withdraws, course deemed unsuitable) Administrative error Non-refundable: You voluntarily withdraw from DR You fail DR (attempt used) You don't appear for assessments 8.9 DR & OTHER POLICIES

question: Can I do DR for prerequisite courses?
answer: Yes, if the course is theory-based. Example: Failed "Data Structures" (prerequisite for "Algorithms") Take DR for Data Structures 83 Once passed, can register for Algorithms Prerequisite clearance via DR is valid.

question: Can I do multiple DR courses simultaneously?
answer: Technically yes, within limits. Limits: 15% of semester credits (usually allows 1 DR course) If you have 2 small-credit courses to recover via DR: May be feasible if both fit within 15% (e.g., 2 courses × 2 credits each = 4 credits, if 15% allows 4) 8.10 DR COMPLETION TIMELINE

question: How long does DR take?
answer: Typical timeline: One regular semester duration 8.11 DR vs. OTHER RECOVERY PATHWAYS 8.11.1: When should I choose DR over Summer Semester? Choose DR if: Course not offered in Summer You prefer independent study You have good self-discipline Summer timeline doesn't work for you (internship, family plans) You want to spread learning over full semester (less intense than 6-week Summer) Choose Summer if: You need structured classroom teaching You lack self-discipline for independent study You want to clear the course quickly (6 weeks vs. 16 weeks) You prefer peer learning environment 84

question: What is the Summer Semester?
answer: An intensive additional academic term conducted during the summer break (typically June-July) to support: Academic recovery (clearing failed/debarred courses) Timely progression (avoiding delays) Acceleration (advanced learners getting ahead) Key features: Shorter duration: ~6 weeks (vs. 16 weeks regular semester) Full course: Complete CA + MCE + ETE (not just exam) Intensive: Condensed timeline, daily classes Fee-based: ₹3,000 per credit

question: Who offers Summer Semester courses?
answer: Schools/Departments decide: Based on student demand Faculty availability Course suitability Not all courses offered every summer—depends on viability.

question: Is Summer Semester mandatory?
answer: Not mandatory, but often essential for: Debarred students (no Supplementary option) Students who failed Supplementary can also opt for Summer Semester Students with prerequisite blocks Students in Academic Year Back (to clear backlogs faster) Think of it as: Highly recommended for recovery, not compulsory. 87

question: Can I take Summer Semester for any reason, or only for recovery?
answer: Multiple purposes: Primary purpose (Recovery): Clear failed courses Clear debarred courses Fulfill prerequisites Secondary purposes (permitted): • Grade improvement • Acceleration (earn credits ahead of schedule) • Pursue double major/minor • Earn additional specializations All are permitted uses, subject to eligibility and approvals. 88 9.2 SUMMER SEMESTER ELIGIBILITY

question: Can I take Summer Semester if I'm in Academic Year Back?
answer: Yes, encouraged. Summer during AYB: • Helps clear backlogs faster • Reduces load in AYB regular semesters • May prevent further delays Your ISP (Individual Study Plan) may include Summer Semester courses as part of recovery strategy. 9.3 SUMMER SEMESTER COURSE LOAD LIMITS

question: How many courses can I take in Summer Semester?
answer: Maximum 2 courses per Summer Semester. This is a strict limit: • Regardless of credit value • Regardless of your academic standing • No exceptions (even for high performers) Reason: Summer is intensive; more than 2 courses would be overwhelming.

question: Can I take 2 courses totalling more than 8 credits?
answer: Yes, as long as it's only 2 courses. Example: • Course A: 4 credits • Course B: 5 credits 90 • Total: 9 credits (2 courses) Allowed The limit is on NUMBER of courses (2), not total credits.

question: Is Summer Semester fee separate from regular semester fees?
answer: Yes, completely separate. You pay: • Regular semester fees (Aug-Dec, Jan-May) • Plus, Summer Semester fees (if you enroll) Summer is an additional cost.

question: Can the Summer fee be waived?
answer: No: Fee is mandatory in all cases

question: Is the Summer Semester fee refundable?
answer: Only in limited cases: Refundable: • University cancels the course (insufficient enrollment) • Verified administrative/payment error Non-refundable: • You withdraw voluntarily You fail the course • You don't attend

question: What is the duration of Summer Semester?
answer: Approximately 6 weeks (intensive). Typical timeline: • Start: Early June • End: Mid-July • Exact dates: Notified by University annually

question: Is Summer Semester online or offline?
answer: Typically, on campus (offline).

question: Do I have to do new CA and MCE, even if I had done them before?
answer: Yes, all components are fresh. No carry-forward: • Previous CA marks: Not carried forward • Previous MCE marks: Not carried forward • You start from scratch Policy states: 93 "No Internal Assessment (IA), Continuous Assessment (CA), or Mid-Course Evaluation (MCE) marks from previous attempts shall be carried forward. Students must complete all prescribed assessment components anew."

question: Is there attendance requirement in Summer Semester?
answer: Yes, 75% attendance is mandatory. Practically: Miss more than 1-1.5 weeks → Risk of debarment in Summer too. Failure to meet attendance: Debarment from Summer End Term Examination → Fail Summer Semester → Recovery attempt wasted.

question: What happens if I'm debarred in Summer Semester too?
answer: You fail the Summer attempt. Consequences: • Summer recovery attempt wasted • Course still failed • If you have another recovery attempt, can try DR/Regular Repeat/ next Summer Semester • If recovery attempts exhausted, must do Regular Course Repeat 9.6 SUMMER SEMESTER WORKLOAD & DIFFICULTY

question: Is Summer Semester easier than regular semester?
answer: No, it's actually HARDER due to intensity. Why harder: • Compressed timeline: 16 weeks → 6 weeks (nearly 3x speed) • Daily classes: No breaks, continuous engagement • Heavy workload: Assignments, MCE, ETE all within 6 weeks • Limited recovery time: If you fall behind, hard to catch up Policy reminder: "Assessment structure and academic standards shall be identical to regular semester norms" So same difficulty content, just delivered faster.

question: Can I do an internship during Summer Semester?
answer: Not recommended. Conflict: 94 • Summer Semester: 5-6 hours classes + 4-6 hours study daily • Internship: Typically 8-9 hours/day • Total: 18+ hours/day (impossible) Choose one: • Summer Semester (if clearing backlogs is priority) • OR internship (if no urgent backlogs) If you have both: • Defer internship to next summer • OR clear backlogs via DR/Regular Repeat instead 9.7 SUMMER SEMESTER OUTCOMES

question: What if I pass Summer Semester?
answer: Course cleared—same as passing regular semester. Outcomes: F grade replaced with passing grade (C to O) • Credits earned • CGPA updated • Prerequisites unlocked (if applicable) • Can progress normally **All attempts (Regular, Supplementary, Summer, Directed Reading, etc.) are maintained in the University’s academic records for audit and regulatory purposes.

question: What if I fail Summer Semester?
answer: Summer recovery attempt wasted, course still failed. What happens: • F grade remains • One recovery attempt used • Must use another recovery pathway: • Directed Reading (if eligible and available) • Summer Semester when available next • Regular Course Repeat • Mandatory Regular Course Repeat, if recovery attempts exhausted. 95

question: Can I retake the same course in next Summer Semester if I fail?
answer: Yes, if: • You have recovery attempt(s) remaining • Course is offered in next Summer • You meet eligibility But: • Each Summer attempt counts toward your 2 recovery attempts max • After exhausting recovery attempts, must do Regular Course Repeat

question: Is there grade capping in Summer Semester?
answer: No grade capping. You can earn any grade (C to O) based on performance. This is fair: Summer is more rigorous (intensive), so no penalty for using it. 9.8 SUMMER SEMESTER & SRIJAN / MANDATORY INTERNSHIP

question: What is SRIJAN Internship?
answer: SRIJAN is UPES's mandatory social internship.

question: Can I defer SRIJAN if I'm taking Summer Semester?
answer: Yes, deferral is permitted. Policy states: "Students enrolled in the Summer Semester may defer SRIJAN / mandatory internship to the next available cycle without penalty, subject to University guidelines" What this means: • If SRIJAN is scheduled for Summer • But you need to attend Summer Semester for recovery • You can defer SRIJAN to next available slot (next summer, or next semester's internship window) • No penalty for deferral 96 9.9 SUMMER SEMESTER & PROGRESSION

question: Does Summer Semester count toward my maximum programme duration (N+2+1)?
answer: YES. Summer is counted as part of total duration: • If you take Summer every year, those weeks add up • Not a "free" recovery time • Plan within N+2+1 limit

question: If I'm in Academic Year Back, does Summer help me exit AYB status?
answer: Yes. How: • AYB triggered by failing ≥50% annual credits • Summer clears some failed courses • Reduces backlog burden • Makes AYB year more manageable (less mandatory backlog load) However: • AYB status is set retrospectively (based on past year performance) • Summer doesn't "undo" AYB trigger • But helps you recover faster during AYB year 97 9.10 OPTING OUT OF SUMMER SEMESTER

question: What is Regular Course Repeat?
answer: Re-enrolling in and repeating a course through the regular semester teaching-learning process (with the next batch of students taking that course). Key features: • Full course repeat: All components (attendance, Continuous Assessments, MidCourse Evaluation, End Term Examination) done fresh • No carry-forward: Nothing from previous attempt(s) is retained • Regular schedule: Same as any regular course offering • With next batch: You join junior students/subsequent batch • No special fee* (part of regular semester fees) *Important: No extra fee within normal programme duration (N). Beyond N, exte

question: Can I do Regular Repeat if I've already tried Supplementary and Summer?
answer: Yes. Example progression: • Regular attempt: F • Recovery attempt 1: Supplementary → F Recovery attempt 2: Summer → F • Next: Regular Repeat (mandatory, since recovery attempts exhausted)

question: Is there a limit on how many times I can do Regular Repeat?
answer: No explicit limit, BUT: Constraints: • Must complete within N+2+1 maximum duration Practically: • You can repeat as many times as needed within the time limit • But endless repetition will exceed N+2+1 → Cannot graduate

question: What if I fail Regular Repeat too?
answer: Course still failed, must repeat again. Cycle continues: • Fail Regular Repeat → Do another Regular Repeat / Summer Semester / DR as applicable • Keep repeating until: o You pass, OR o You exceed N+2+1 (cannot continue) 10.5 REGULAR REPEAT & PROGRAMME PROGRESSION

question: Can I take new courses while doing Regular Repeat?
answer: Yes, subject to: • Maximum Credit limit of the semester • Prerequisite clearance • No timetable clash

question: Does Regular Repeat count toward semester credit limit ?
answer: Yes. Unlike Summer/DR (which are separate), Regular Repeat is part of regular semester registration.

question: Can I do Regular Repeat while in Academic Year Back?
answer: Yes, Regular Repeat is one of the pathways used during AYB. AYB framework: Mandatory backlog registration Regular Repeat is the mechanism for those backlogs (unless using Summer/DR) So being in AYB often means multiple Regular Repeats.

question: If I'm in AYB, is Regular Repeat my only option?
answer: No. You may utilise any approved recovery pathway (Supplementary Examination, Summer Term, Directed Reading, or Regular Repeat), subject to eligibility criteria and as specified in your approved Individual Study Plan (ISP). 106 SECTION 11: COURSE SUBSTITUTION POLICY (AG-10) 107 11.1 UNDERSTANDING COURSE SUBSTITUTION

question: What is Course Substitution?
answer: Replacing a failed optional/elective course with a different course from the same category, instead of repeating the same failed course. Key points: • Only for optional courses (electives or exploratory courses) • NOT for core/mandatory courses • Replaces permanently (cannot go back to original course) • Subject to approval (not automatic) Think of it as: Swapping out a failed elective for a different elective.

question: Does substitution count toward my recovery attempts?
answer: Yes, substitution uses one recovery attempt. Attempt counting: 109 • Regular attempt: Failed "Elective A" • Recovery attempt 1: Substitute with "Elective B" → Pass • Used 1 recovery attempt If you fail "Elective B" too: • Recovery attempt 1 wasted • Must use Recovery attempt 2 (another substitution, or repeat "Elective B") 11.3 SUBSTITUTION FOR GRADE IMPROVEMENT

question: Can I substitute an elective to improve my grade?
answer: Yes, but strict limits apply. Rules for substitution for grade improvement: 1. Only ONE substitution for grade improvement allowed in entire programme. Policy states: "Only one (1) course substitution for grade improvement is permitted during the entire duration of the programme." 2. The substituted course cannot be substituted again or repeated. Meaning: • Once you substitute for improvement, that's permanent • If you fail the new elective, you're stuck with F 3. Same category requirement: • Major Elective → Major Elective only • Exploratory → Exploratory only

question: Can my substitution request be denied?
answer: Yes. Reasons for denial: 1. Course not eligible: • Core course (not substitutable) • Prerequisite course (must clear the specific prerequisite) 2. Category mismatch: • Trying to substitute Major Elective with Exploratory (not same category) 3. Programme structure violation: • Substitution would violate credit requirements (e.g., programme requires min 3 Finance electives, you've substituted 2 already, can't substitute the 3rd) 4. Capacity/availability: • Substitute course full (no seats) • Substitute course not offered 5. Excessive substitutions: • Dean may deny if you're substituting too many

question: Is there a fee for Course Substitution?
answer: No separate substitution fee. Policy states: "Course substitution shall not attract any additional or separate fee. The substituted course shall be treated as a regular course registration." Fees: • Substitute course fee: Included in regular semester fees (no extra charge) 112 • No "substitution processing fee"

question: How does substitution appear on my transcript?
answer: Yes. All substitution attempts are formally recorded and retained in the University’s academic records.

question: Does substitution affect CGPA?
answer: Yes, positively (if you pass substitute course). CGPA impact: • Failed course: F grade (0.0 grade points) removed • Substitute course: New grade (e.g., B+ = 7.0) added • CGPA recalculated This is why substitution can be strategic for CGPA recovery. 113 SECTION 12: GRADE IMPROVEMENT POLICY (AG -11) 114

question: What is Grade Improvement?
answer: A voluntary attempt to replace a previously earned passing grade (C or higher) with a higher grade to boost your CGPA. Key points: • Not for failed courses (that's recovery, not improvement) • Optional (you already passed, just want better grade) • Limited attempts (strict rules) • Risk involved (could get lower grade)

question: Can I improve any passing grade (C, C+, B, B+, A, A+)?
answer: Yes, any passing grade. Even if you got A+, you could theoretically try to improve to O (if you're 1-2 marks short). However: • Keep risk-benefits in mind • Improving from A+ to O may not be worth effort/risk

question: Can I improve grades from first year while I'm in final year?
answer: Yes, as long as you're still enrolled. Example: • Year 4 student • Want to improve Year 1 course grade (got C, want A) • Eligible (if you haven't improved it before) • Keep maximum credit limit in mind

question: Will grade improvement delay my graduation?
answer: Depends on when you do it: If done BEFORE final semester: • No delay (improved grades ready before graduation) If done IN final semester: • Could delay if improvement result comes after degree conferral deadline If done through Regular Repeat AFTER graduating: • Not possible (must be enrolled) Best practice: • Complete all improvements by pre-final year • Don't risk graduation delay

question: Can I improve non-credit courses (Audit, S/U grades)?
answer: No. Grade improvement applies to credit-bearing graded courses only. Non-credit courses: • Audit courses (AP/AF) - no grades • S/U courses - no grade points • Cannot be "improved"

question: What if the course curriculum changed since I took it?
answer: You take the current version of the course. Implications: • Syllabus may be different • You study new syllabus (not old) 118 SECTION 13: AUDIT COURSE POLICY (AG -12) 119

question: What is an Audit Course?
answer: A course taken purely for learning enrichment without earning academic credit or affecting your CGPA. Key characteristics: • Non-credit: Doesn't count toward degree requirements • Non-CGPA: Doesn't affect your grades • Voluntary: Completely optional • Learning-focused: For personal/professional development • No academic risk: Can't fail in a way that hurts progression Think of it as: Sitting in on a class to learn, without the pressure of grades.

question: Can I audit a course from a different School/programme?
answer: Yes, this is actually encouraged (interdisciplinary learning). Example: • You're a BTech CSE student • Want to audit "Marketing Management" from School of Business • Permitted (subject to seat availability and approvals) This is the beauty of audit—explore beyond your major.

question: How many courses can I audit per semester?
answer: No explicit limit stated in policy. However: • Practical limit based on time (each audit course requires attendance) • Schools may impose limits (e.g., max 2 audits per semester) • Approval authority (Dean) may deny if too many, citing too much academic load on the student Typical: 1-2 audit courses per semester is reasonable if you do not have backlogs

question: Can my audit registration be denied?
answer: Yes, for several reasons: 1. No seat availability: • Course is full with regular students • No room for auditors 2. Course not suitable for audit: • Lab/studio/internship type • Assessment-intensive (defeats audit purpose) 3. You don't meet prerequisites: • Course requires background you don't have • You wouldn't be able to follow along 4. Instructor denial: • Instructor doesn't want audit students (perhaps due to confidential material, small seminar format, etc.) 5. Academic overload concern: • Dean feels you're taking too many courses (regular + audit) • Could affect your performance Audit i

question: Do I have to attend audit courses?
answer: YES. 75% attendance is mandatory. Same as regular courses: • Must attend ≥75% of classes • If attendance <75% → Audit Fail (AF) Why attendance matters even though it's audit: • You're taking a seat in class • Instructor is investing time • Serious learning requires engagement Audit is NOT: Sign up and never attend.

question: Do I have to take exams in audit courses?
answer: No, audit courses have NO formal exams (CA, MCE, ETE). Assessment is participation-based only. However: 124 • Instructor MAY require you to attempt some quizzes/assignments for learning (not graded) • This is for your benefit (to check understanding) If instructor mandates exam-like assessments for auditors: • It's unusual and you can discuss with Dean • Audit should be low-pressure

question: What if I can't attend 75% due to timetable clash with my regular courses?
answer: Then don't audit that course. Audit courses must fit your schedule: • No special accommodations • Clashes are your responsibility If clash occurs after registration: • Withdraw early from audit

question: Do AP and AF affect my CGPA?
answer: NO. Zero CGPA impact. AP and AF are: Academic audit outcomes Not grades Carry no credit Carry no grade points 125 Your CGPA calculation completely ignores audit courses.

question: Is there a fee to audit courses?
answer: Ordinarily, audit courses are offered without fee. Policy states: "Audit courses are ordinarily offered without fee." However: "The University reserves the right to prescribe a nominal audit fee, if required, with approval of the competent authority and prior notification."

question: Can I drop an audit course mid-semester?
answer: Yes, and there's no penalty.
