Since audit is: • Non-credit • Non-CGPA • Voluntary Withdrawal consequences: • No fee refund (if fee was charged) • AF may be recorded for that semester (but won't show on final transcript) • No academic penalty Process: • Inform instructor • Notify APO to remove from record Best practice: Withdraw early if you realize you can't commit (don't waste seat). SECTION 14: INTERNAL ACADEMIC MOBILITY POLICY (AG- 13) (Change of Programme / Change of Major / Change of Specialisation) 127 14.1 UNDERSTANDING ACADEMIC MOBILITY

question: What is Internal Academic Mobility?
answer: Formal processes allowing you to change your academic structure within UPES, limited to: Three types of changes: 1. Change of Programme: Degree-level change (BBA → BTech) 2. Change of Major: Disciplinary change within same degree (BTech CSE → BTech Mechanical) 3. Change of Specialisation: Track change within same major (Finance → Marketing within BBA)

question: Why does UPES allow academic mobility?
answer: Alignment with NEP 2020 principles: • Students discover interests during studies • Initial choice may not be right fit • Support informed decision-making UPES philosophy: Better to switch and succeed than persist in wrong path and fail.

question: What is credit mapping?
answer: Academic equivalence exercise to determine if previously earned credits align with new programme/major/specialisation requirements. All courses completed under the previous structure shall be reviewed against the requirements of the revised curriculum to determine academic equivalence. The evaluation will consider alignment of learning outcomes, content coverage, assessment rigor, credits, and contact hours. Based on this review, each course will be classified as Equivalent (fully transferable), Partially Equivalent (may require bridging requirements), or Not Equivalent (treated as surplus or

question: Who determines credit equivalence?
answer: Two-level evaluation: Level 1: Faculty Subject Matter Expert: • Faculty from target programme/major • Evaluates content and learning outcomes • Makes equivalence recommendation Level 2: School Approval: • Programme Lead / Cluster Head reviews • Dean approves or denies All decisions documented in Credit Mapping & Equivalence Form.

question: Is credit transfer automatic?
answer: NO. Policy explicitly states: "No automatic or blanket credit transfer shall be permitted." Every single course evaluated individually. Even if courses have similar names: • "Data Structures" in CSE vs. "Data Structures" in IT 130 • May have different depth, tools, focus • Equivalence not assumed

question: Can credit mapping dilute programme learning outcomes?
answer: NO. Policy prohibits this: Credit mapping SHALL NOT: • Dilute Programme Outcomes • Bypass mandatory core courses • Skip prerequisite chains Example of NOT allowed: • You completed "Basic Physics" (2 credits, introductory) • Want it mapped to "Engineering Physics" (4 credits, advanced) • Denied (not equivalent in depth/rigor) 131

question: Can late-stage changes be restricted?
answer: YES. Reasons for late-stage denial: • Insufficient time to complete new requirements • Curriculum feasibility (too many prerequisites unmet) • Would exceed N+2+1 duration Policy states: "Late-stage requests may be restricted or denied due to curriculum feasibility." "Late stage" typically means: • After Semester 4 (for 4-year programmes) 132 • After Semester 6 (for 5-year integrated programmes)

question: What if my change would extend beyond N+2+1?
answer: Automatically denied. Policy explicitly: "No change shall be permitted if it results in extension beyond maximum programme duration (N+2+1)." Example: • 4-year BTech, you're in Semester 5 • Want to change to BTech CSE (different major) • Evaluation shows: Would need 6 more years to complete • Total: 5 years done + 6 more = 11 years • Exceeds: N+2+1 = 4+2+1 = 7 years • Denied 14.5 GOVERNANCE & APPROVAL STRUCTURE

question: How long does the approval process take?
answer: Around 2 weeks. Apply early in application window to allow processing time.

question: Can I appeal if my request is denied?
answer: Yes, but only on procedural grounds. Valid appeal grounds: • Procedural error (application not reviewed properly) • Data error (credits incorrectly mapped) NOT valid appeal grounds: • Academic judgment ("I think the courses are equivalent even though Dean said no") • Preference ("I really want to change") • Expectation ("I thought I'd be approved") Appeal process: Submit to Pro-Vice Chancellor (within 15 days of denial).

question: Will I be informed of the decision?
answer: Yes, formal communication via: • Email to your official UPES email • ERP notification • Letter from Dean's office (if approved) Timeline: Usually within 2-4 weeks of application. 134 14.6 CHANGE OF PROGRAMME (DEGREE -LEVEL CHANGE)

question: What is Change of Programme?
answer: Switching from one degree to another, resulting in different qualification. Examples: • BBA → BTech • BA Economics → BSc Data Science • BTech Civil → BBA • MBA → MTech Degree on certificate changes.

question: What if significant credit loss occurs?
answer: You acknowledge and accept credit loss. Mandatory Student-Parent Undertaking includes: "Student acknowledges that significant credit loss may occur as a result of Change of Programme." Example loss scenario: • BTech (1 year): 44 credits earned • Change to BArch (Architecture) • Credit mapping: Only 8 credits map (English, Math basics) • Lost: 36 credits (engineering courses irrelevant to architecture) You're informed before approval—no surprises.

question: What is Change of Major?
answer: Disciplinary shift within same degree programme, keeping the same degree but changing primary field of study. Examples: • BTech Computer Science → BTech Mechanical Engineering • BBA Marketing → BBA Finance 138 • BA Economics → BA Psychology Degree stays same (BTech, BBA, BA), major changes.

question: Does programme duration reset for Change of Major?
answer: NO. You remain in same degree programme. Duration calculation: • Original BTech admission: 2024 • BTech duration: N = 4 years • Maximum: 4+2+1 = 7 years from 2024 • Change major in 2025 (Semester 2) • Duration: Still 7 years from 2024 (no reset) 139

question: Do I have to complete new major's core courses?
answer: YES, all of them. Policy states: "Core courses of the new major that were not previously undertaken must be completed." Cannot bypass core requirements. Example: • Change to BTech Mechanical • Must complete ALL Mechanical core courses: o Thermodynamics o Fluid Mechanics o Machine Design o Manufacturing Processes o Etc. • Even if you completed CSE cores (irrelevant)

question: Does credit mapping bypass prerequisite chains?
answer: NO. Policy explicitly: "Credit mapping shall not bypass prerequisite chains." Example: • BTech Mechanical requires: 140 o Engineering Mechanics (Year 1) o Strength of Materials (Year 2, needs Engineering Mechanics) o Machine Design (Year 3, needs Strength of Materials) If you change to Mechanical in Semester 3: • Even if you have credits from CSE • You must complete the prerequisite chain properly • Can't skip to Machine Design without prerequisites This may delay graduation if prerequisites span multiple years.

question: What is Change of Specialisation?
answer: Change between approved specialisation tracks within same major and degree. Examples: • BTech CSE with Specialisation in AI → BTech CSE with Specialisation in Cybersecurity • BBA Finance (Corporate Finance specialisation) → BBA Finance (Investment Banking specialisation) Degree and Major stay same, only specialisation track changes.

question: What do I need to complete after Change of Specialisation?
answer: All prescribed specialisation-specific courses. Progression: • Complete remaining common major courses (already on track) • Complete ALL new specialisation courses • Old specialisation courses (not mapped) don't count Transcript reflects new specialisation only upon full completion. 142

question: Can I change specialisation in final year?
answer: Depends on specialisation course distribution. Feasible if: • Specialisation courses concentrated in final year • Minimal difference between specialisations Not feasible if: • Specialisation courses span multiple years • Already completed most old specialisation courses (wasted) School evaluates case-by-case. 14.9 ACADEMIC MOBILITY PROCESS

question: Are there fees for Change of Programme/Major/Specialisation?
answer: No separate "change fee" stated in policy. However: • Regular semester fees: Continue as per new programme/major • If new programme has different fee structure: You pay new structure • Bridge courses: o Taken in regular semester: Included in semester fees o Taken via Summer/DR: ₹3,000/credit applies Example: • Change from BBA (fees ₹80,000/semester) to BTech (fees ₹1,20,000/semester) • From next semester: Pay BTech fees

question: Will my scholarship continue after changing programme/major?
answer: Depends on scholarship terms. Possible outcomes: 1. Scholarship continues: • If scholarship is programme-agnostic (e.g., merit-based on CGPA) 2. Scholarship discontinued: • If scholarship was programme-specific (e.g., "Engineering Merit Scholarship") • New programme may not qualify 3. New scholarship application: • You may apply for scholarships in new programme • Subject to eligibility 146 SECTION 15: CHANGE OF MINOR POLICY (AG -14) 147 15.1 UNDERSTANDING CHANGE OF MINOR

question: What is a Minor?
answer: A structured set of courses (typically 24 credits) pursued outside your Major discipline, reflected on transcript and degree certificate only upon full completion. Example Minors: • BTech CSE student pursuing Minor in Business Analytics • BBA Finance student pursuing Minor in Data Science • BA Economics student pursuing Minor in Psychology Key feature: Interdisciplinary learning.

question: What is Change of Minor?
answer: Formal process to discontinue one approved Minor and opt for a different Minor. Example: • Declared Minor in Business Analytics (Semester 1) • Completed 8 credits • Realize Data Science Minor more aligned with career goals • Apply for Change of Minor (Business Analytics → Data Science)

question: Who does this policy apply to?
answer: Students admitted from Academic Year 2026–27 onwards.

question: Is a Minor mandatory?
answer: Minor is optional (students can choose) 15.2 CHANGE OF MINOR ELIGIBILITY

question: How many times can I change my Minor?
answer: Once. 151 Choose carefully.

question: Can I drop my Minor entirely (not replace)?
answer: Yes, your courses will be mapped as exploratory and you will not earn a minor. 15.3 CREDIT TREATMENT AFTER CHANGE OF MINOR

question: Do surplus credits count toward graduation?
answer: NO. Surplus credits: • Remain visible on academic record • Do NOT contribute toward: o Degree credit requirement o Minor completion o Major requirements • Simply documented Example: • Programme requires 160 credits • You have 165 credits (5 surplus from old Minor) • Graduation requirement: Still 160 (surplus doesn't add)

question: What is "academic equivalence review" for credit mapping?
answer: Faculty evaluation to determine if a course from old Minor is equivalent to new Minor requirements. Process: • Submit course syllabi • Faculty compare: o Learning outcomes o Content coverage o Assessment rigor • Decide: Equivalent or Not Equivalent 15.4 IMPACT ON DEGREE & TRANSCRIPT

question: Will my old Minor appear on transcript?
answer: NO. Only completed Minors appear. Example: • Started Business Analytics Minor (completed 8 credits) Changed to Data Science Minor (completed full 24 credits) Transcript shows: Minor in Data Science only No mention: Business Analytics 153

question: What about those 8 Business Analytics credits?
answer: They appear as individual courses (not as part of Minor). Transcript: • Shows courses: "Intro to Business," "Marketing Fundamentals" (from old Minor) • Classification: Exploratory credits or surplus non-contributory credits • Not grouped under "Minor" 15.5 CHANGE OF MINOR APPROVAL PROCESS

question: Can my Change of Minor request be denied?
answer: Yes. Reasons: 1. Application beyond deadline (after Semester 3): • Hard deadline, no exceptions 2. Feasibility issues: • Cannot complete new Minor in time • Would require exceeding N+2+1 3. Prerequisite blocks: • Cannot meet prerequisites for new Minor • Would delay graduation 4. Seat unavailability: • Target Minor full 5. Late-stage (≥12 credits completed): • Automatic denial 6. Student unwilling to commit to Summer/DR (if required): • If mid-stage change requires Summer/DR • Student says "I can't/won't do Summer" • Denial (change not feasible otherwise) 15.6 FEES & FINANCIAL IMPACT

question: Is there a fee for Change of Minor?
answer: No separate Change of Minor fee. However: If Summer Semester or DR required (mid-stage change): • Summer: ₹3,000/credit • DR: ₹3,000/credit 155 This is disclosed before approval—you acknowledge and accept.

question: What if Change of Minor delays graduation?
answer: Extension fees may apply (if beyond N years). Scenario: • 4-year programme • Change of Minor + Summer courses → Complete in Year 5 • Year 5: Extension fees Disclosed in undertaking before approval. 156 SECTION 16: LATERAL ENTRY & INTER-INSTITUTIONAL MIGRATION (AG-15) 157 16.1 UNDERSTANDING LATERAL ENTRY & MIGRATION

question: What is Lateral Entry?
answer: Admission into an ongoing undergraduate programme at UPES (typically Semester 3 or later) based on prior learning completed at another recognized institution. Common pathways: • Diploma holders entering BTech • Advanced Diploma holders entering BBA/BA/BSc • Certificate programme completers entering UG programmes Key feature: You "skip" Year 1, enter directly into Year 2 or 3.

question: What is Inter-Institutional Migration?
answer: Transfer of a currently enrolled UG student from another recognized university into the same or equivalent programme at UPES, typically after completing Semester 1 or 2. Example: • Student at University X completes Semester 1 of BTech CSE • Wants to transfer to UPES BTech CSE • Applies for migration • If approved, continues at UPES from Semester 2 Key feature: You're already in a degree programme, moving mid-stream.

question: What is "Level 5" and "Level 6"?
answer: National Qualifications Framework (NQF) levels: • Level 5: Undergraduate Certificate (equivalent to 1 year of UG study) • Level 6: Undergraduate Diploma (equivalent to 2 years of UG study) • Level 7: Bachelor's Degree (3-4 years) Under NEP 2020: You can exit at Level 5 or 6 and later re-enter.

question: Can I enter at Semester 2 (mid-Year 1)?
answer: Not under Lateral Entry. For Semester 2 entry: • Only through Migration (if you completed Semester 1 at another university) 159 • Lateral Entry: • Typically Semester 3 (Year 2) or Semester 5 (Year 3)

question: Can Diploma holders enter BTech at Semester 5 (Year 3)?
answer: Depends on Diploma type and credits: 3-year Diploma (after 10th): • Equivalent to ~Level 6 (2 years UG) • Entry: Semester 3 (Year 2) typically Advanced Diploma / 4-year Diploma: • May have more credits • Entry: Could be Semester 5 (Year 3) if credits justify University evaluates case-by-case based on credit equivalence. 16.3 LATERAL ENTRY ELIGIBILITY

question: Can international students apply for Lateral Entry?
answer: Yes, subject to: • Equivalence of foreign qualification (verified by Association of Indian Universities or equivalent) • Valid student visa • Meet same academic eligibility

question: What if I have backlogs in my Diploma?
answer: Not eligible until backlogs cleared. Policy states: "The applicant has no unresolved academic backlogs or failures at the source institution at the time of final admission confirmation." Process: • Clear all Diploma backlogs first • Then apply for Lateral Entry

question: Can I get relaxation in eligibility?
answer: Yes, 10% relaxation for outstanding sportspersons. 161 Policy states: "A relaxation of up to 10% in eligibility may be granted to outstanding sportspersons, subject to applicable UGC/Sports Authority of India guidelines and approval of the Admissions Supervision Committee." Example: • BTech requires 60% in Diploma • Outstanding sportsperson has 54% (6% short) • May be granted admission (within 10% relaxation) Requirements: • Recognized sports achievements (national/international) • SAI/sports body certification • Committee approval 16.4 MIGRATION ELIGIBILITY

question: Is there a separate quota for Migration?
answer: No fixed quota. Migration: Strictly vacancy-based. Example: • BTech CSE: 5 students withdrew (5 vacancies) 163 • Migration allowed: Up to 5 students (if all apply from same programme level) 16.6 UNIVERSITY DISCRETION

question: Is admission guaranteed if I meet eligibility?
answer: NO. Policy explicitly states: "Admission through Lateral Entry or Inter-Institutional Migration is not an entitlement. Meeting minimum eligibility criteria does not guarantee admission." University reserves right to: • Approve or reject based on academic equivalence • Consider capacity constraints • Apply regulatory requirements • Prioritize based on institutional academic priorities

question: What if multiple students apply for limited vacancies?
answer: Merit-based selection. Process: 1. All eligible applicants shortlisted 2. Ranked by: a. Prior academic performance (Diploma %, UG CGPA) b. Entrance test/interview scores (if conducted) c. Credit equivalence strength 3. Top rankers admitted until vacancies filled 16.7 CREDIT TRANSFER & EQUIVALENCE FRAMEWORK

question: What are Bridge Courses?
answer: Structured academic interventions to address credit, content, or prerequisite gaps between your prior learning and UPES curriculum. Purpose: • Fill knowledge gaps • Meet prerequisite requirements • Ensure you're prepared for advanced courses Q16.8.2: When are Bridge Courses prescribed? Scenarios: 1. Partial equivalence (50-69% similarity): • Course partially maps • Bridge module fills gap 2. Credit deficit: • Your course had fewer credits than UPES equivalent • Bridge adds missing credit 3. Content gaps: • Your course missed some topics • Bridge covers those topics 167 4. Prerequisite preparat

question: Are Bridge Courses mandatory?
answer: YES, if prescribed. Policy: "Bridge courses prescribed under this Policy shall be designed and delivered by the respective School, and academically equivalent to regular courses." Meaning: • Full academic rigor • Attendance, assessment, grading apply • Must pass to progress Cannot skip or bypass bridge courses.

question: Do prerequisite rules apply to Lateral Entry/Migration students?
answer: YES, strictly. Policy: "UPES follows a strict prerequisite-based academic progression model. Prerequisite alignment shall be verified for all mapped courses and future registrations. Prerequisites shall not be waived under any circumstances." No exceptions.

question: Can I register for a course without clearing prerequisite?
answer: NO. Policy: "Students shall not be permitted to register for a course unless prerequisites are fulfilled." Even for Lateral Entry students, this is enforced. 16.10 ATTENDANCE, ASSESSMENT & ACADEMIC REGULATIONS

question: Do Lateral Entry/Migration students follow same attendance rules?
answer: YES. No relaxation. Policy states: "All attendance, assessment, grading, condonement, and debarment rules shall be governed by: UPES Attendance Policy, UPES Attendance Condonement Policy, UPES Assessment & Evaluation Policy. No relaxation shall be permitted solely due to Lateral Entry or Migration status." 75% attendance mandatory (same as regular students).

question: Are assessment standards different for us?
answer: NO. Same standards. Same: • CA + MCE + ETE structure • Passing criteria (35% UG, 40% PG) • Grading (absolute/relative) • Re-evaluation rights No leniency for being a Lateral Entry/Migration student. 16.11 MAXIMUM DURATION OF STUDY

question: What if I can't complete within maximum duration?
answer: Cannot continue the programme. Options: • Exit with earned credits (Certificate/Diploma under NEP 2020) • Discontinue without degree Plan carefully to graduate within limit. 16.12 STUDENT & PARENT/GUARDIAN UNDERTAKING

question: What is Break of Study?
answer: A temporary suspension of academic enrollment where you remain enrolled at UPES but in inactive academic status for one or more full semesters. Also called: • Semester Break • Semester Withdrawal • Temporary Withdrawal Key characteristics: • You DON'T drop out (still enrolled) • You DON'T attend classes or take courses • You CAN return after the break • Your academic record is preserved • However, break of study duration will get counted towards N+2+1 norm Think of it as: Academic pause button.

question: Who can apply for Break of Study?
answer: • All full-time UG and PG students at UPES • Across all Schools

question: What is Re-evaluation?
answer: A process where your End-Term Examination answer script is evaluated again by a different evaluator. Key features: • Only for End-Term Exams (not CA, not MCE) • Fresh evaluation (different evaluator than original) • May result in grade increase, decrease, or no change

question: What is the fee for Re-evaluation?
answer: ₹2,000 per course. Payment: • Online via ERP • Mandatory at application time Refund: Generally NOT refundable • Even if grade doesn't change

question: If I'm satisfied with Re-evaluation outcome, what happens next?
answer: Matter closed. You accept the revised (or unchanged) grade and move on.

question: What if I'm still dissatisfied after Re-evaluation?
answer: You can submit a grievance (not another re-evaluation). This is the "Post Re-evaluation Grievance" process. 18.2 POST RE-EVALUATION GRIEVANCE

question: What is Post Re-evaluation Grievance?
answer: A grievance raised AFTER re-evaluation if you're still dissatisfied with the outcome. Key point: This is NOT a second re-evaluation. Policy states: "This grievance does not constitute a second re-evaluation and does not involve fresh assessment of the answer script." So: • No third evaluator • No re-marking • Just a review of the re-evaluation process

question: Is Post Re-evaluation Grievance decision final?
answer: YES, absolutely final. Policy: "The decision taken after grievance redressal by the University-level committee shall be final and binding. The student shall be informed of the final outcome as per University procedure. No further appeal, representation, or petition shall be entertained after this stage in relation to the same examination." No further appeals, no exceptions. 18.3 ACADEMIC APPEALS (NON-EXAMINATION)

question: What are Academic Appeals?
answer: Appeals related to non-examination academic decisions, limited strictly to: • Procedural errors • Administrative or clerical errors • Data or record-related errors • Incorrect application of published academic rules 183 • NOT for: • Academic judgment Quality of teaching • Policy relaxation

question: Do I have to go through all three levels?
answer: YES, sequential escalation is mandatory. Cannot skip: • Can't go directly to Dean without trying faculty first • Can't bypass Programme Lead Rationale: Most issues resolve at lower levels; escalation only if genuinely unresolved.

question: What is Academic Integrity?
answer: Ethical principles of honesty, trust, fairness, respect, and responsibility in all your academic work—learning, assignments, exams, and research. In simple terms: • Do your own work • Give credit when using others' ideas • Don't cheat on exams • Don't lie to get academic advantages • Follow the rules Think of it as: Playing fair in the academic game.

question: Why does UPES care so much about Academic Integrity?
answer: Three main reasons: 1. Your degree's value: • If students cheat, UPES degrees lose credibility • Employers won't trust UPES graduates • Your hard-earned degree gets devalued 2. Fairness to honest students: • Students who work hard deserve fair competition • Cheaters steal opportunities from honest students 3. Your own development: • Cheating prevents genuine learning • You won't develop real skills • Long-term career impact (you won't actually know your field) UPES commitment: Upholding the highest standards so your degree means something. 196

question: Is this policy meant to punish students?
answer: No, it's corrective and educative. Policy states: "This policy is corrective and educative in intent, while remaining firm against actions that compromise academic integrity." Meaning: • For minor mistakes: Warning + education (learn proper citation) • For serious misconduct: Penalties (to maintain standards and deter others) Goal: Help students learn to work with integrity, not just punish.

question: What is the "Fit-to-Sit Principle"?
answer: If you take an exam, you're declaring you're fit to take it. Policy states: "A student who appears for an assessment is deemed fit to sit, unless exceptional circumstances prevented prior disclosure." Meaning: If you sit for an exam: • You can't later say "I was sick, unfair exam" • You can't claim exam conditions were poor (unless truly exceptional) • You accepted the conditions by appearing Exception: Genuine emergencies (earthquake during exam, sudden medical crisis) that prevented prior disclosure. Why this rule exists: • Prevents students from appearing, seeing questions, then claiming "I

question: What is "Academic Judgment" and why can't it be appealed?
answer: Academic judgment = Faculty's professional evaluation of your work quality. Definition: "Professional evaluation of academic work by faculty based on approved rubrics and learning outcomes. Academic judgment is not appealable." Examples of academic judgment: • Grading an essay (15/20 vs. 18/20) • Evaluating a presentation (B+ vs. A) • Assessing quality of a project • Determining if an answer is correct/partially correct Why not appealable under this policy: • Faculty are experts in their field • They use approved rubrics and learning outcomes • Judgment is subjective but professional 198 • All

question: Can I ever challenge a grade?
answer: Yes, but not through this policy. For grade challenges (without misconduct): • Use Re-evaluation process (for End-Term exams) • Use Academic Appeals process (for CA, attendance, procedural errors) • Governed by: Academic Appeals & Re-evaluation Policy (AG-17) For misconduct-related grade challenges: • If you're accused of cheating and given F, you CAN appeal the misconduct allegation • Use this policy's appeals mechanism • But you cannot appeal the faculty's academic judgment of quality 199 19.3 CATEGORIES OF ACADEMIC MISCONDUCT

question: What if I didn't know something was misconduct?
answer: Ignorance is not an excuse, but it matters for penalty. Policy approach: • First-time unintentional violation (you genuinely didn't know): Likely Level A (warning + education) 200 • Repeated violation (you've been warned before): Escalates to Level B or higher Your responsibility: • Attend academic integrity workshops (mandatory in Year 1 at most universities) • Read assignment instructions carefully • Ask faculty if unsure Example: • First time improper citation (didn't know APA format) → Warning + workshop • Second time (after workshop) → F grade (you should know by now)

question: Can I get in trouble for helping a friend cheat?
answer: YES. Facilitating misconduct is also misconduct. Examples: • Letting friend copy your exam → Both guilty (you for allowing, friend for copying) • Sharing your assignment for friend to "reference" (they copy) → Both guilty • Taking exam for someone else (impersonation) → Both guilty (severe) Policy term: "Assistance" or "Facilitation" Don't help others cheat, even out of kindness—you'll be punished too. 19.4 UNFAIR MEANS (UFM) DURING EXAMINATIONS

question: What is Unfair Means (UFM)?
answer: Any act during an exam that compromises fairness or exam integrity. Definition: "Any act, attempt, or assistance by a student during mid-term, end-term, practical, laboratory, or online examinations that compromises examination integrity." Covers: • Mid-term exams (MCE) • End-term exams (ETE) • Practical/lab exams • Viva exams • Online/remote-proctored exams 201

question: Does "attempting" UFM count, even if I didn't succeed?
answer: YES. Attempt = actual commission. Policy explicitly states: "[Note: Attempted UFM shall be treated at par with committed UFM.]" Examples: Scenario A: Possession without use • You bring cheat sheet to exam, hidden in pocket • Invigilator finds it before you use • Outcome: Still UFM (possession is enough, even if unused) Scenario B: Caught while copying • You're trying to see neighbor's paper • Invigilator catches you before you write anything • Outcome: Still UFM (attempt counts) Why: Deterrence. Can't claim "I was just about to, didn't actually do it."

question: What if I bring my phone to exam but don't use it—is that UFM?
answer: Depends on rules and circumstances. If exam rules clearly state "No phones allowed": • Bringing phone (even switched off, in bag) = UFM (possession) • Penalty: Likely Level U1 (minor UFM, written warning, script cancellation) If exam rules don't explicitly prohibit: • Gray area, but generally exams prohibit electronic devices by default • Still risky Best practice: • Leave phone outside exam hall (with friend, in locker, at home) • Don't risk it If you accidentally bring phone: • Immediately surrender to invigilator before exam starts • Document the surrender • Usually no penalty (you disclose

question: Can I get in trouble for UFM in online exams?
answer: YES, same rules apply. Policy explicitly covers: "Online, hybrid, remote-proctored, and take-home examinations" Common UFM in online exams: • Using unauthorized tabs/websites • Getting help from someone off-camera • Looking at notes off-screen • Using AI tools (ChatGPT, etc.) if prohibited • Screen-sharing with friends Detection: • AI proctoring software (tracks eye movement, detects other faces, monitors screen) • Faculty may review recordings • Patterns in answers (very similar to online sources) Don't assume online = easy to cheat. Technology catches a lot.

question: Will I be informed if UFM is suspected against me?
answer: YES, you have right to be informed and heard. Due process: • Written notice of allegation • Evidence shared (what was confiscated, what was observed) • Opportunity to respond (hearing before committee) You cannot be punished without: 1. Being told what you're accused of 2. Seeing the evidence 3. Getting a chance to explain Timeline: Usually within 1-2 weeks of detection. 204

question: What if I disagree with the UFM allegation?
answer: You can defend yourself at the hearing. Your rights: Present your side of the story • Provide counter-evidence (if any) • Explain circumstances Example defense: • "The invigilator thought I was looking at neighbor's paper, but I was looking at the clock on the wall behind them. Here's a photo showing the clock location." However: • Your defense must be credible • Evidence is key (your word vs. invigilator's word—evidence matters) If defense succeeds: UFM case dismissed. If defense fails: Penalty imposed.

question: How serious is UFM—will I be expelled?
answer: Depends on severity. UFM has 3 levels (U1, U2, U3). Summary Table: Level Type Examples Penalties U1 Minor UFM Possession without use, first procedural violation Warning, answer script cancelled U2 Major UFM Copying, using phone, repeated U1 F grade, no Supplementary for that course U3 Severe UFM Impersonation, organized cheating, tampering Semester cancelled, suspension, possible expulsion Most UFM cases are U1 or U2. U3 is rare but very serious.

question: Why does UPES have a Student Discipline Policy?
answer: UPES maintains a structured discipline framework to ensure: • A safe, respectful, and inclusive academic environment • Protection of student dignity and institutional integrity • Responsible behaviour within and outside campus Discipline is considered an academic responsibility and part of holistic character development.

question: Does off-campus behaviour come under University discipline?
answer: Yes. Misconduct occurring during internships, tours, public events, social media usage, or any activity linked to UPES affiliation may be treated as disciplinary violations. 20.2 CODE OF CONDUCT EXPECTATIONS

question: What behaviour is expected from students?
answer: Students are expected to: • Maintain respectful conduct with faculty, staff, and peers • Follow all University rules and lawful instructions • Avoid behaviour that brings disrepute to the University • Contribute to a positive academic environment

question: Is discipline only about punishment?
answer: No. The Code of Conduct aims to promote: • Accountability • Ethical behaviour • Mutual respect • Academic focus Corrective actions may include counselling or educational tasks in addition to disciplinary measures. 20.3 TYPES OF MISCONDUCT

question: What are the categories of misconduct?
answer: Acts of indiscipline are broadly classified as: • Classroom Misconduct • Minor Acts of Indiscipline / General Misconduct • Major Acts of Indiscipline • Zero-Tolerance Offences • Off-Campus Misconduct • Failure to Appear Before Authorities Each category has different severity levels and competent authorities.

question: What is Classroom Misconduct?
answer: Examples include: • Disrupting lectures • Proxy attendance • Unauthorized mobile use • Disrespectful behaviour toward faculty or classmates Action may be taken by the concerned faculty member.

question: What are Minor Acts of Indiscipline?
answer: These include violations affecting campus order but not considered grave misconduct, such as: • Dress code violations • Smoking or vaping within campus • Noise disturbance • Disobedience of instructions These may be handled by the Proctorial Board or Chief Proctor.

question: What are Major Acts of Indiscipline?
answer: Serious misconduct affecting safety or institutional functioning, including: • Property damage • Forgery or misuse of documents • Cyber misconduct or hacking • Physical assault or harassment • Mass bunk or exam boycott These cases may lead to severe disciplinary actions.

question: What are Zero-Tolerance Offences?
answer: These are grave violations attracting immediate strict action, such as: • Ragging in any form • Sexual harassment • Violence or fights • Possession or supply of banned substances • Discriminatory harassment Such offences may result in suspension, rustication, or expulsion. 20.4 DISCIPLINARY AUTHORITIES & PROCESS

question: Who handles disciplinary complaints?
answer: Depending on severity: • Faculty Member – Classroom misconduct • Proctorial Board / Chief Proctor – Minor and major misconduct • Disciplinary Committee / University Authorities – Severe cases Q20.4.2: What happens after a complaint is reported? A: Typically: 1. Preliminary review by Proctorial authority 2. Inquiry or hearing 3. Recommendation of disciplinary action 4. Issuance of order by competent authority Students are expected to cooperate with the process.

question: What if a student does not appear before disciplinary authorities?
answer: Failure to appear may lead to: • Suspension from classes • Blocking of attendance portal • Parent/guardian intimation Access is restored only after compliance. 20.5 DISCIPLINARY ACTIONS & PENALTIES

question: What types of disciplinary actions can be imposed?
answer: Depending on severity: • Verbal or written warning • Counselling or corrective assignments • Debarment from classes or activities • Suspension • Withholding scholarships • Rustication or expulsion

question: Can disciplinary actions affect academics or placements?
answer: Yes. Certain penalties may include: • Debarring from examinations • Loss of academic privileges • Restrictions on internships or placements 20.6 DISCIPLINARY PROBATION

question: What is disciplinary probation?
answer: Disciplinary probation is a formal warning period during which a student’s conduct is closely monitored. It may be imposed: • Up to six months by Chief Proctor • Beyond six months with Vice Chancellor approval Q20.6.2: What happens during probation? A: During probation: • Behaviour is monitored by Proctor/Course Coordinator • Parents may be informed • Participation in events may require prior approval Students continue attending classes but remain under observation.

question: What kind of behaviour may lead to probation?
answer: Examples include: • Public misconduct • Social media defamation • Repeated disruption • Misconduct during industrial visits or events 20.7 APPEAL & REVIEW

question: Can a student appeal against a disciplinary decision?
answer: Yes. Students may submit a written appeal to the Final Appellate Committee (FAC) within 10 days of receiving the punishment order.

question: Does filing an appeal stop the disciplinary action?
answer: Not automatically. Unless specifically stayed by the competent authority, the disciplinary order remains effective during review. 20.8 IMPORTANT CLARIFICATIONS

question: Is ragging allowed in any form?
answer: No. Ragging is strictly prohibited and treated as a cognizable offence under applicable regulations.

question: Can social media behaviour lead to disciplinary action?
answer: Yes. Online misconduct, defamatory posts, or misuse of digital platforms linked to UPES may be treated as disciplinary violations.

question: What if an act is not listed explicitly in the Policy?
answer: Any behaviour deemed a violation of discipline by competent authority may still attract action. Absence of a listed example does not imply permission. 20.9 SEEKING HELP OR CLARIFICATION Students may contact: • Proctorial Board / Chief Proctor Office • School Discipline Controller • Dean’s Office for guidance or official clarification.
